Transaction date November 15: Debit Telephone Service Expense $457.00; Credit Accounts Payable $457.00. Transaction date November 25: Debit Accounts Payable $457.00; Credit Cash $457.00.
October 31: Debit Prepaid Expense $457.00; Credit Accounts Payable $457.00. November 25: Debit Accounts Payable $457.00; Credit Prepaid Expense $457.00.
Transaction date October 31: Debit Telephone Service Expense $457.00; Credit Accounts Payable $457.00. Transaction date November 25: Debit Accounts Payable $457.00; Credit Cash $457.00.
1-8.e: In accrual accounting, expenses are recognized when the revenues for which they were incurred are recognized, called the matching principle. Or, if the expenses cannot be directly related to the production of revenues, for example telephone service, the expenses are recognized when the good or service is received and liability for payment is incurred. Therefore, the expense is recognized immediately when incurred, so on October 31, the last day of the service period, Telephone Service Expense is debited to increase it and Accounts Payable is credited to increase it. When the invoice is paid on November 25, Accounts Payable is debited to decrease it and Cash is credited to decrease it.

wrongWhy.0: In accrual accounting, expenses are recognized when the revenues for which they were incurred are recognized, called the matching principle. Or, if the expenses cannot be directly related to the production of revenues, for example telephone service, the expenses are recognized when the good or service is received and liability for payment is incurred. That date is October 31, the last day of the service period. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.1: Even though November 15, the date the invoice is received, may be the date the invoice is entered into the accounting system, the transaction date and the date when the expense is recognized should be October31, the last day of the service period. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.2: The event described is not a prepaid expense, and this set of transactions would recognize neither the telephone service expense nor the reduction to cash when payment is made. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-8.q: JK Industries sold inventory for $300,000 that had cost JK Industries $200,000 to Row Products on June 25, 20X4, and Row Products picked up the inventory the same day. Payment from Row Products was due on July 25, 20X4, and Row Products paid for the inventory on July 24, 20X4. JK uses a perpetual inventory system. How should the sale to Row Products be reflected in JK Industries’ June 30, 20X4 financial statements?
o: As credits of $300,000 to sales revenue and accounts receivable and debits of $200,000 to cost of goods sold and inventory.
No transaction should be posted, since Row Products had not paid as of June 30.
As increases of $300,000 to sales revenue and accounts receivable, as an increase of $200,000 to cost of goods sold, and as a decrease of $200,000 to inventory.
As an increase to cost of goods sold and a decrease to inventory. When the cash is received in July, it should be recorded as an increase in cash and an increase in sales revenue.
1-8.e: The sale should be reported on the June 30, 20X4 financial statements as an increase (credit) to sales revenue of $300,000, an increase (debit) to accounts receivable of $300,000; an increase (debit) to cost of goods sold; and a decrease (credit) to inventory of $200,000.

wrongWhy.0: Assets are debited to increase them and credited to decrease them. Therefore, accounts receivable needs to be debited, not credited, for $300,000, and inventory needs to be credited, not debited, for $200,000. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.1: A transaction needs to be posted to recognize the sales revenue, the related cost of sales, the receivable, and the reduction in inventory. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.3: Revenue is recognized when the customer obtains control of the asset, which Row products did on June 25, so the revenue should be recognized along with the cost of goods sold on that date. Also as of June 25, the cash expected to be received in 30 days should be reflected on the June 30 financial statements as an increase in accounts receivable. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-8.q: A company makes sales totaling $15,600 on credit. The cost of the inventory sold is $6,700, and the company uses a perpetual inventory system. How should the sale be accounted for?
o: Debit accounts receivable $15,600, credit sales revenue $15,600.
Debit accounts receivable $15,600, credit inventory $6,700, credit gross profit $8,900.
Debit accounts receivable $15,600, credit sales revenue $15,600; debit cost of goods sold $6,700, credit inventory $6,700.
Debit cost of goods sold $6,700, debit inventory $8,900, credit sales revenue $15,600.
1-8.e: The sales totaled $15,600 and the sales were made on credit, so accounts receivable is debited $15,600 to increase it while sales revenue is credited for $15,600 to increase it. When a perpetual inventory system is used, the cost of inventory that is sold is removed from the inventory account and charged to cost of goods sold expense whenever a sale is made. Therefore, cost of goods sold is debited for the cost of the inventory, $6,700, to increase expense, and inventory is credited for $6,700 to reduce inventory.

wrongWhy.0: This records the sales revenue and the receivable, but it fails to record the reduction in inventory and the increase in cost of goods sold expense. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.1: Gross profit is not an account that can be debited or credited. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.3: Inventory needs to be reduced by the cost of the inventory sold and cost of goods sold needs to be increased by the cost of the inventory sold. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-8.q: What is the impact on the accounting equation when a current period expense is paid?
o: Both sides of the equation decrease.
Neither side of the equation changes.
a. Both sides of the equation increase.
Only the asset side of the equation changes.
1-8.e: The accounting equation is Assets = Liabilities + Equity. In accrual accounting, an expense is recorded by debiting an expense account and crediting a liability account such as accounts payable. When the payment is made, accounts payable is debited to decrease the liability and cash is credited to decrease cash. Therefore, the impact on the accounting equation when a current period’s expense is paid is to decrease assets on the left side of the equals sign and decrease liabilities on the right side of the equals sign. Note: If a cash accounting system is in use (which is not generally accepted accounting principles), the answer is the same: both sides of the equation decrease. The payment would be recorded by debiting an expense account, which would decrease equity, and crediting cash, which would decrease assets.

wrongWhy.1: The accounting equation is Assets = Liabilities + Equity. When a current period expense is paid, the accounting equation will change. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.2: The accounting equation is Assets = Liabilities + Equity. Both sides of the equation do not increase when a current period expense is paid. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.3: The accounting equation is Assets = Liabilities + Equity. More than the asset side of the equation changes when a current period expense is paid. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-8.q: During the month of October, a company purchased 1,000 units of inventory for $500 per unit and sold 900 of these units which represented 10% of the company’s annual sales budget in units. The company also incurred administrative costs of $300,000 during October. By applying the matching principle, the total amount of the company’s expenses on its October income statement is
1-8.e: The matching principle requires that a company recognize the expenses that generate revenue in the same period that the revenue is recognized. Or, in some casesd, a company should recognize revenue in the same period that the costs that generate that revenue are expensed. For this question, the company needs to recognize two expenses. The first is the cost of goods sold for the 900 units that were sold. These 900 units were purchased for $500 each, for a total of $450,000. The second expense is the $300,000 of administrative costs that need to be expensed as they are incurred. These two expenses together total $750,000.

wrongWhy.0: This answer recognizes the cost of all 1,000 units purchased as an expense, not only for the 900 units sold.
wrongWhy.2: This is not the correct answer. Please see the correct answer for an explanation. We have been unable to determine how to calculate this incorrect answer choice. If you have calculated it, please let us know how you did it so we can create a full explanation of why this Include the full Question ID number and the actual incorrect answer choice -- not its letter,
wrongWhy.3: This is not the correct answer. Please see the correct answer for an explanation. We have been unable to determine how to calculate this incorrect answer choice. If you have calculated it, please let us know how you did it so we can create a full explanation of why this Include the full Question ID number and the actual incorrect answer choice -- not its letter, because that can change with every study session created. The Question ID number appears at the top of the question. Thank you in advance for helping us to make your HOCK study materials better.

1-8.q: When an expense is incurred in the process of providing a product to a customer, the expense is properly recognized
o: when the related revenue is recognized.
when the liability to make payment is recognized.
when the cash is paid.
when an order for the product is received.
1-8.e: According to the matching principle, when an expense is incurred in the process of providing goods or services to customers, the expense is properly recognized when the revenue for which the expense was incurred is recognized.

wrongWhy.1: When an expense is incurred in the process of providing a product to a customer, the expense is not properly recognized when the liability to make payment is recognized. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.2: When an expense is incurred in the process of providing a product to a customer, the expense is not properly recognized when the cash is paid. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.3: When an expense is incurred in the process of providing a product to a customer, the expense is not properly recognized when an order for the product is received. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-8.q: On April 10, Glenoak Lighting Products received a request from Entertaining Events for a special spotlight that Glenoak does not normally carry in inventory and thus needed to be specially ordered. The price of the spotlight was $4,000 and its cost to Glenoak Lighting was $2,500. Glenoak’s policy for special order items is to require a 50% deposit before the item can be ordered. Entertaining Events paid the required $2,000 deposit on April 12. How should Glenoak Lighting account for the deposit?
o: April 12: Debit Prepaid Expense $2,000; Credit Cash $2,000.
April 12: Debit Cash $2,000, Credit Sales Revenue $2,000.
April 12: Debit Deferred Revenue $2,000; Credit Cash $2,000.
April 12: Debit Cash $2,000, Credit Deferred Revenue $2,000.
1-8.e: The $2,000 received cannot be recognized as sales revenue until the performance obligation to the customer has been satisfied, that is, when the customer obtains control of the asset. So until the customer receives the spotlight, the advance deposit must be recognized by Glenoak as a liability because Glenoak has an obligation to fulfill the order. When the deposit is received, Glenoak debits Cash and credits Deferred Revenue, a liability account, for the amount received.

wrongWhy.0: The receipt of an advance deposit is not recorded by debiting the asset, Prepaid Expense. An advance deposit received is not a prepaid expense. Crediting Cash, an asset, decreases it, and Cash needs to be increased, not decreased. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.1: The $2,000 received cannot be recognized as revenue until the performance obligation to the customer has been satisfied, that is, when the customer obtains control of the asset. When the deposit is received on April 12, the customer has not yet obtained control of the spotlight, so Sales Revenue cannot be credited. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.2: This transaction is backwards. Crediting an asset decreases it, and Cash needs to be debited to increase it, not credited to decrease it. Debiting Deferred Revenue, a liability, decreases the liability, and the liability needs to be credited to increase it, not debited to decrease it. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-8.q: A company receives an electric utility bill for $2,500 for electricity used from November 1 through November 30. The bill is paid on December 15. How should the bill be accounted for?
o: As of December 15, debit utility expense for $2,500 and credit accounts payable for $2,500.
As of December 15, debit utility expense for $2,500 and credit cash for $2,500.
As of November 30, debit accounts payable for $2,500 and credit utility expense for $2,500. As of December 15, debit cash for $2,500 and credit accounts payable for $2,500.
As of November 30, debit utility expense for $2,500 and credit accounts payable for $2,500. As of December 15, debit accounts payable for $2,500 and credit cash for $2,500.
1-8.e: The expense is accrued as of the date it was incurred, so the expense account is debited and the accounts payable account is credited for the amount of the bill on November 30. When the bill is paid on December 15, accounts payable is debited and cash is credited for the amount paid.

wrongWhy.0: The expense should be accrued in the period in which it was incurred, which was November. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.1: This is consistent with the cash method of accounting. The cash method is not aligned with generally accepted accounting principles, so that is not the way the bill should be accounted for. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.2: These entries are both backwards. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-8.q: What is the impact on the accounting equation when a sale is made?
o: Both sides of the equation decrease.
Both sides of the equation increase.
Neither side of the equation changes.
Only the asset side of the equation changes.
1-8.e: The accounting equation is Assets = Liabilities + Equity. When a sale is made using the perpetual inventory method, the sale is posted as follows: Dr Accounts Receivable or Cash for the sale amount Dr Cost of Goods Sold for the cost of the sale Cr Sales Revenue for the sale amount Cr Inventory for the cost of the sale Assets are increased by the sale amount and decreased by the cost of the sale. Equity is increased by the sales revenue and decreased by the cost of the sale. Therefore, both assets and equity increase by the amount of the gross profit on the sale, which is the difference between the sales revenue and the cost of the sale.

wrongWhy.0: The accounting equation is Assets = Liabilities + Equity. When a sale is made, both sides of the equation do not decrease. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.2: When a sale is made, the accounting equation will change. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.3: The accounting equation is Assets = Liabilities + Equity. More than the asset side of the equation will change. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-8.q: When cash for sales that have been made on credit is collected, how should the receipt of the cash be recorded in the accounting system?
o: Debit cash, credit accounts receivable.
Debit sales revenue, credit cash.
Debit cash, credit sales revenue.
Debit accounts receivable, credit cash.
1-8.e: The cash received is a debit to cash and a credit to accounts receivable. When a credit sale is made, accounts receivable is debited to increase it, and sales revenue is credited to increase it. When the cash from the sale is collected, cash is debited to increase it and accounts receivable is credited to decrease it.

wrongWhy.1: Sales revenue would not be debited, and cash would not be credited. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.2: The cash received is debited to cash, but sales revenue is not credited because sales revenue would have been credited when the credit sales were made. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.3: This is backwards. A debit increases an asset ledger account and a credit decreases an asset ledger account. So this transaction would increase accounts receivable and decrease cash, which is the opposite of what is needed. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-8.q: Under the accrual basis of accounting, revenue is recognized when:
o: Cash is received from the customer.
The customer places an order.
It is earned, regardless of when cash is received.
The invoice is mailed to the customer.
1-8.e: Under accrual accounting, revenue is recognized when it is earned — that is, when goods are delivered or services are performed — regardless of when cash is received. This matches revenues with the period in which they are generated.

wrongWhy.0: Recognizing revenue when cash is received describes the cash basis of accounting, not accrual.
wrongWhy.1: A customer order creates no revenue. Revenue is earned when the performance obligation is fulfilled.
wrongWhy.3: Mailing an invoice does not constitute earning revenue. Revenue is recognized upon delivery of goods or services.

1-8.q: A company provides consulting services worth $12,000. The client pays $5,000 immediately and agrees to pay the remaining $7,000 next month. What is the correct journal entry?
o: Debit Cash $5,000; Credit Revenue $5,000.
Debit Cash $5,000; Debit Accounts Receivable $7,000; Credit Service Revenue $12,000.
Debit Cash $12,000; Credit Service Revenue $12,000.
Debit Accounts Receivable $12,000; Credit Service Revenue $12,000.
1-8.e: The full $12,000 of revenue is earned when services are provided. The $5,000 received is Cash (debit), and the $7,000 still owed is Accounts Receivable (debit). Total debits of $12,000 equal the full revenue credit of $12,000.

wrongWhy.0: Only recording $5,000 understates revenue by $7,000. The full $12,000 is earned when services are performed.
wrongWhy.2: Only $5,000 cash was received — debiting Cash $12,000 overstates the cash received.
wrongWhy.3: The $5,000 already received should be recorded as Cash, not Accounts Receivable.

1-8.q: Under the matching principle, expenses should be recognized:
o: When cash is paid.
At the end of each fiscal year.
In the same period as the revenues they help generate.
When the expense is approved by management.
1-8.e: The matching principle requires that expenses be recognized in the same period as the revenues they help generate. This ensures that the income statement accurately reflects the cost of generating the period's revenues.

wrongWhy.0: Recognizing expenses when cash is paid describes cash basis accounting. Accrual accounting matches expenses to revenue periods.
wrongWhy.1: Expenses are matched to the period they are incurred, not just at year-end.
wrongWhy.3: Management approval is an internal control procedure, not an accounting recognition criterion.

1-8.q: A company pays $24,000 on January 1 for a two-year advertising campaign. How much advertising expense should be recorded each month?
o: $24,000 immediately on January 1.
$12,000 per year, recognized at year-end only.
$1,000 per month over 24 months.
$2,000 per month over 12 months.
1-8.e: $24,000 ÷ 24 months = $1,000 per month. The prepaid advertising is an asset that is expensed as the benefit is used over the two-year campaign period.

wrongWhy.0: Expensing the full $24,000 immediately violates the matching principle — the benefit extends over 24 months.
wrongWhy.1: Recognizing only at year-end and on an annual basis does not properly match the expense to each monthly period.
wrongWhy.3: $2,000 per month would expense the full amount in 12 months, but the campaign runs for 24 months.

1-8.q: Which of the following is an example of an accrued expense?
o: Rent paid in advance for the next three months.
Wages earned by employees but not yet paid at period-end.
Revenue received from customers before services are performed.
Depreciation recorded on manufacturing equipment.
1-8.e: Accrued expenses are expenses incurred (and a liability created) before cash is paid. Wages earned but unpaid at period-end are a classic accrued expense — the company owes employees money that has not yet been disbursed.

wrongWhy.0: Rent paid in advance is a prepaid expense (asset), not an accrued expense. Cash is paid before the benefit is used.
wrongWhy.2: Revenue received before services are performed is unearned revenue (a liability), not an accrued expense.
wrongWhy.3: Depreciation is an allocation of a previously paid cost, not an accrued expense involving an unpaid obligation.

1-8.q: A customer returns merchandise worth $800 that was originally purchased on account. What is the correct journal entry on the seller's books?
o: Debit Cash $800; Credit Accounts Receivable $800.
Debit Sales Returns $800; Credit Accounts Receivable $800.
Debit Accounts Receivable $800; Credit Sales Returns $800.
Debit Sales Revenue $800; Credit Cash $800.
1-8.e: A sales return reduces both the customer's obligation (Accounts Receivable decreases — credit) and revenue (Sales Returns & Allowances increases — debit). Sales Returns is a contra-revenue account that reduces net sales.

wrongWhy.0: No cash is involved — the original sale was on account. Accounts Receivable should be reduced (credited).
wrongWhy.2: This is reversed. Accounts Receivable decreases (credit) and Sales Returns increases (debit).
wrongWhy.3: The return should be recorded as a reduction in Accounts Receivable, not a cash payment.

1-8.q: At year-end, a company has earned $3,000 of interest on a bank deposit but has not yet received the cash. What adjusting entry is required?
o: Debit Cash $3,000; Credit Interest Revenue $3,000.
Debit Interest Receivable $3,000; Credit Interest Revenue $3,000.
Debit Interest Expense $3,000; Credit Interest Payable $3,000.
No entry — revenue is only recorded when cash is received.
1-8.e: Under accrual accounting, interest earned but not yet received is an accrued revenue. The adjusting entry debits Interest Receivable (an asset) and credits Interest Revenue to recognize the income in the period it was earned.

wrongWhy.0: Cash has not yet been received — debiting Cash overstates the cash balance.
wrongWhy.2: Interest Expense and Interest Payable are used when a company owes interest, not when it has earned interest.
wrongWhy.3: Under accrual accounting, revenue is recognized when earned, not when cash is received.

1-8.q: A company records $15,000 of salary expense at month-end for employees who will be paid next month. Which accounts are affected?
o: Debit Cash $15,000; Credit Salaries Expense $15,000.
Debit Salaries Expense $15,000; Credit Cash $15,000.
Debit Salaries Expense $15,000; Credit Salaries Payable $15,000.
Debit Salaries Payable $15,000; Credit Salaries Expense $15,000.
1-8.e: Since salaries are earned by employees but not yet paid, this is an accrued expense. Salaries Expense increases (debit) and Salaries Payable (a liability) increases (credit). When employees are paid next month, Salaries Payable will be debited and Cash credited.

wrongWhy.0: Cash is not received by the company when it records salary expense. Cash is paid to employees.
wrongWhy.1: Cash has not yet been paid. The liability Salaries Payable should be credited, not Cash.
wrongWhy.3: This is reversed — expenses increase with debits, not credits. Payables increase with credits.

1-8.q: Which of the following transactions results in both an asset and a revenue being recorded simultaneously?
o: Receiving cash in advance from a customer for future services.
Performing services for a customer who pays cash immediately.
Purchasing inventory on account.
Paying wages to employees.
1-8.e: When services are performed and cash is received immediately, Cash (an asset) increases and Service Revenue increases at the same time. This is a cash revenue transaction where the asset and revenue are recorded in the same entry.

wrongWhy.0: Receiving cash in advance creates Cash (asset) and Unearned Revenue (liability) — not revenue. Revenue is recognized later when services are performed.
wrongWhy.2: Purchasing inventory on account increases Inventory (asset) and Accounts Payable (liability) — no revenue is involved.
wrongWhy.3: Paying wages creates Salaries Expense (increases) and Cash (decreases) — no asset increase or revenue is recorded.

1-8.q: A retail store sells merchandise for $5,000 cash. The cost of the merchandise was $3,000. What is gross profit from this transaction?
1-8.e: Gross profit = Sales Revenue - Cost of Goods Sold = $5,000 - $3,000 = $2,000. Gross profit measures the profit earned before operating expenses are deducted.

wrongWhy.0: $5,000 is sales revenue, not gross profit. Gross profit is after deducting the cost of the goods sold.
wrongWhy.1: $3,000 is the cost of goods sold, not gross profit.
wrongWhy.3: $8,000 would result from adding revenue and cost together — gross profit is the difference, not the sum.

1-9.q: On October 1, 20X3, the board of directors of Discounts4U Company declared a cash dividend of $0.50 per share, payable on November 15, 20X3, to common stockholders of record as of November 1, 20X3. The company had 80,000 common shares outstanding as of October 1. How will the declaration of the dividend affect Discounts4U’s balance sheet and/or income statement?
o: Net Income is decreased by $40,000 and Equity is decreased by $40,000 on October 1, 20X3.
No journal entry is required until the dividend is paid on November 15, 20X3.
Liabilities are increased by $40,000 and Equity is decreased by $40,000 on October 1, 20X3.
Cash is decreased by $40,000 and Equity is decreased by $40,000 on November 1, 20X3.
1-9.e: The amount of the dividend is $40,000 (80,000 shares × $0.50 per share). On the date the dividend is declared, Equity is decreased and Dividends Payable, a liability account, is increased by the amount of the dividend.

wrongWhy.0: Net Income is not decreased by a dividend paid to stockholders. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.1: A journal entry is required on the date the dividend is declared. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.3: Neither Cash nor Equity will be decreased on November 1. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-9.q: Investment instruments with maturity dates of less than 90 days when purchased that can be easily converted into cash without a significant loss in value are classified on the balance sheet as
o: convertible bonds.
equity securities.
cash equivalents.
foreign currency.
1-9.e: Very liquid, short-term investment instruments with a maturity date of less than 90 days when they are acquired that are easily converted into known amounts of cash without significant loss in value are classified on the balance sheet as cash equivalents.

wrongWhy.0: A convertible bond is a bond that can be converted by the bondholder into a stated number of shares of the issuer’s common stock at any time during the term of the bond. Investment instruments with maturity dates of less than 90 days when purchased would not be classified as convertible bonds. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.1: Equity securities are common or preferred stocks in other companies held as investments. Common stocks do not have maturity dates, so investment instruments with maturity dates of less than 90 days when purchased cannot be classified on the balance sheet as equity securities. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.3: Foreign currency is not an investment security with a maturity date, so investment instruments with maturity dates of less than 90 days when purchased cannot be classified on the balance sheet as foreign currency. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-9.q: On July 1, 20X4, NLF Corporation pays an insurance premium of $12,000 for insurance coverage on its facilities from July 1, 20X4 to June 30, 20X5. What is the impact of the payment on the accounting equation for NLF Corporation on July 1, 20X4?
o: No change.
Assets, $(12,000); liabilities, $(12,000).
Assets, $12,000; equity, $12,000.
1-9.e: A payment made in advance of receiving the good or service is a prepaid expense. It is recorded by debiting the asset account Prepaid Expenses and crediting Cash for the amount of the payment. Prepaid Expenses and Cash are both asset accounts. Therefore, the amount of the increase in total assets caused by the debit to Prepaid Expenses is equal to the amount of the decrease in total assets caused by the credit to Cash. The result is no change in total assets, and of course no change in either total liabilities or total equity, either.

wrongWhy.1: Total assets will not decrease by $12,000 and total liabilities will not decrease by $12,000. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.2: Total assets will not increase by $12,000 and total equity will not increase by $12,000. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.3: Total assets will not decrease by $12,000 and total equity will not decrease by $12,000. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-9.q: A company makes a $1,000 mortgage payment every month to pay for its manufacturing plant; $200 of this payment is for interest on the loan. What is the correct journal entry to record this transaction?
o: Debit: building $1,000; credit: loans payable $1,000.
Debit: loans payable $800; debit: interest expense $200; credit: cash $1,000.
Debit: loans payable $1,000; credit: cash $1,000.
Debit: cash $1,000; credit: loans payable $1,000.
1-9.e: Loans payable is a liability account, and it needs to be decreased by $800, the amount of the principal payment made. Liability accounts are decreased by debits, so loans payable needs to be debited for $800. Interest expense is an expense account, and expense accounts are increased by debits. Interest expense needs to be increased by $200 to reflect the interest paid, so interest expense needs to be debited for $200. Cash is an asset, and assets are decreased by credits. Cash needs to be decreased by $1,000, the total amount paid. So, cash needs to be credited for $1,000.

wrongWhy.0: A payment made on a mortgage loan does not affect the asset account that contains the original cost of the building securing the loan. Loans payable is a liability, and liabilities are increased by credits. However, the loan payable balance should be decreased by the amount of the principal payment made on the loan, not increased by the amount of the payment. A portion of the payment made was a payment of interest. This journal entry does not include any recognition of the interest that was paid.
wrongWhy.2: A portion of the payment made was a payment of interest. This journal entry does not include any recognition of the interest that was paid. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.3: Cash is an asset, and assets are increased by debits. Debiting cash would cause cash to increase, whereas cash has, in fact, decreased. Loans payable is a liability, and liabilities are increased by credits. Crediting loans payable would cause the balance of the liability to increase, whereas the principal balance of the liability has, in fact, decreased. Interest was paid. A portion of the payment should be recorded as interest expense, but that has not happened in this journal entry. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-9.q: The correct category in the chart of accounts for the Prepaid Expenses account is
o: Assets
Liabilities
Expenses
Equity
1-9.e: Prepaid Expenses is an asset account. A prepayment is a payment made in advance of receiving any benefit from the payment. The benefit from the payment will be received over a future period. Therefore, the advance payment is not charged to an expense account until the benefit is received. When the payment is made, an asset account, Prepaid Expenses, is debited to increase it. The cost will be expensed over the future period as the benefit from it is received.

wrongWhy.1: Prepaid Expenses is not a liability account. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.2: Prepaid Expenses is not an expense account. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.3: Prepaid Expenses is not an equity account. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-9.q: TBS Inc. purchases an automated mail folder and inserter machine for $10,000. The company makes a $1,000 down payment on the equipment and finances the remaining $9,000 on a fiveyear loan secured by the equipment. How should the purchase be recorded?
o: Debit Equipment for $10,000, credit Cash for $1,000, credit Long-term Debt for $9,000.
Debit Cash for $1,000, debit Long-term Debt for $9,000, credit Equipment for $10,000.
Debit Long-term Debt for $9,000, credit Equipment for $9,000.
Debit Equipment for $9,000, credit Long-term Debt for $9,000
1-9.e: Equipment and Cash are both assets, and assets are increased by debits and decreased by credits. Therefore, Equipment should be debited for $10,000, the cost of the machine, and Cash should be credited for $1,000, the amount of the down payment paid. Long-term Debt is a liability, and liabilities are increased by credits. Therefore, Long-term Debt is credited for $9,000, the amount of the loan.

wrongWhy.1: Cash and Equipment are both assets, and assets are increased by debits and decreased by credits. Cash should be decreased to record the $1,000 down payment made, not increased; and Equipment should be increased to record the historical cost of the machine purchased, not decreased. Long-term Debt is a liability, and liabilities are increased by credits and decreased by debits. Long-term Debt should be increased by the amount of the loan, not decreased. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.2: The decrease to Cash because of the down payment made is not reflected in this transaction. Long-term Debt is a liability, and liabilities are increased by credits and decreased by debits. Long-term Debt should be increased by the amount of the loan, not decreased. Equipment is an asset, and assets are increased by debits and decreased by credits. Equipment should be increased to record the historical cost of the machine purchased, not decreased.
wrongWhy.3: The decrease to Cash for the $1,000 down payment made is not reflected in this transaction, and the amount by which the Equipment account is increased does not reflect the full historical cost of the equipment. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-9.q: The Dividends account is
o: A contra-expense account used for recording dividends declared.
A contra-equity account used for recording dividends declared.
A contra-revenue account used for recording dividends paid.
An expense account used for recording dividends paid.
1-9.e: The Dividends account is an account in the Equity section of the balance sheet. It is contra to the Retained Earnings account, and it is where dividends to be paid are recorded when they are declared by the Board of Directors. Because it is a contraaccount, it carries a balance that is the opposite of the normal balance of Equity accounts, and it reduces the value of Retained Earnings in Equity.

wrongWhy.0: The Dividends account is not a contra-expense account used for recording dividends declared. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.2: The Dividends account is not a contra-revenue account used for recording dividends paid. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.3: The Dividends account is not an expense account used for recording dividends paid. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-9.q: On December 10, PET Company received an order from YU Industries for $5,000 for items costing PET $2,000. Since the items required a special order, PET requested and received a deposit from YU in the amount of $1,500 on December 12. How should the deposit be recorded in the accounting system?
o: Debit Cash for $1,500 and credit Deferred Revenue for $1,500.
Debit Accounts Receivable for $3,500, debit Cash for $1,500, credit Sales Revenue for $5,000.
Debit Accounts Receivable for $3,500, credit Sales Revenue for $5,000, credit Deferred Revenue for $1,500.
Debit Accounts Receivable for $3,500, credit Cash for $1,500, credit Sales Revenue for $5,000.
1-9.e: When a deposit is received in advance, it should be recorded by debiting Cash and crediting a liability account, Deferred Revenue, for the amount received. It is not recognized as sales revenue until the performance obligation to the customer is satisfied, which will not occur until the special order item is provided to the customer.

wrongWhy.1: The revenue from the sale and the receivable should not be recognized until the performance obligation to the customer is satisfied, which will not occur until the special order item is provided to the customer. The receipt of the advance payment creates a liability for PET. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.2: The total debits do not equal the total credits in this transaction; and the revenue from the sale and the receivable should not be recognized until the performance obligation to the customer is satisfied, which will not occur until the special order item is provided to the customer. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.3: The total debits do not equal the total credits in this transaction; crediting Cash will reduce cash, not increase it; and the revenue from the sale and the receivable should not be recognized until the performance obligation to the customer is satisfied, which will not occur until the special order item is provided to the customer. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-9.q: DFI Inc. rents a warehouse for which it pays rent of $120,000 per year, payable in advance. On January 1, 20X4, DFI makes its 20X4 rent payment in cash. How should the rent payment be recorded in the accounting system on January 1?
o: Debit Prepaid Expenses $120,000, credit Cash $120,000
Debit Prepaid Expenses $110,000, Debit Rent Expense $10,000, Credit Cash $120,000
Debit Rent Expense $120,000, credit Accounts Payable $120,000
Debit Rent Expense $120,000, credit Cash $120,000
1-9.e: The full amount of the payment should be debited to the asset account, Prepaid Expenses, and credited to Cash on January 1. As of January 1, none of the warehouse facilities had yet been used, so it is not appropriate to expense any of the payment until usage occurs and benefit is received from the payment.

wrongWhy.1: As of January 1, none of the warehouse facilities had yet been used, so it is not appropriate to expense any of the payment until usage occurs and benefit is received from the payment. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.2: As of January 1, none of the warehouse facilities had yet been used, so it is not appropriate to expense any of the payment until usage occurs and benefit is received from the payment. Furthermore, the payment was made in cash, so it is not appropriate to credit accounts payable, since accounts payable is a liability account and a credit transaction increases a liability. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.3: As of January 1, none of the warehouse facilities had yet been used, so it is not appropriate to expense any of the payment until usage occurs and benefit is received from the payment. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-9.q: A company declares and pays a $5,000 cash dividend to its shareholders. Which of the following correctly records this transaction?
o: Debit Dividends Expense $5,000; Credit Cash $5,000.
Debit Retained Earnings $5,000; Credit Cash $5,000.
Debit Cash $5,000; Credit Dividends Payable $5,000.
Debit Dividends $5,000; Credit Retained Earnings $5,000.
1-9.e: When a dividend is declared and paid simultaneously, Retained Earnings decreases (debit) and Cash decreases (credit). Dividends reduce retained earnings because they distribute accumulated profits to shareholders.

wrongWhy.0: Dividends are not an expense — they are a distribution of earnings to owners and reduce Retained Earnings, not an expense account.
wrongWhy.2: Cash is paid out (credited), not received. Dividends Payable would be used if the dividend was declared but not yet paid.
wrongWhy.3: This is reversed — dividends decrease Retained Earnings (debit RE), not increase it.

1-9.q: A company purchases its own shares of stock (treasury stock) for $20,000 cash. What is the effect on the balance sheet?
o: Total assets increase $20,000; Total equity increases $20,000.
Total assets decrease $20,000; Total equity decreases $20,000.
Total assets remain the same; Total liabilities decrease $20,000.
Total equity increases $20,000; Total liabilities decrease $20,000.
1-9.e: Purchasing treasury stock reduces Cash (assets decrease by $20,000) and reduces stockholders' equity (Treasury Stock, a contra-equity account, increases by $20,000). The accounting equation remains balanced: both assets and equity decrease by the same amount.

wrongWhy.0: Buying back shares does not increase assets or equity — it reduces both Cash and net stockholders' equity.
wrongWhy.2: Liabilities are not affected by a treasury stock purchase. Assets and equity both decrease.
wrongWhy.3: Equity decreases, not increases, when treasury stock is purchased.

1-9.q: A company issues common stock at $15 per share when the par value is $1 per share. For 1,000 shares issued, what is recorded?
o: Debit Cash $1,000; Credit Common Stock $1,000.
Debit Cash $15,000; Credit Common Stock $15,000.
Debit Cash $15,000; Credit Common Stock $1,000; Credit Additional Paid-in Capital $14,000.
Debit Cash $15,000; Credit Retained Earnings $15,000.
1-9.e: Cash received = 1,000 shares × $15 = $15,000. Common Stock is recorded at par value: 1,000 × $1 = $1,000. The excess above par ($15,000 - $1,000 = $14,000) is recorded as Additional Paid-in Capital.

wrongWhy.0: This records only the par value portion and ignores the $14,000 excess received above par.
wrongWhy.1: Recording the full $15,000 to Common Stock overstates the par value of the stock by $14,000.
wrongWhy.3: Retained Earnings represents accumulated earnings, not proceeds from stock issuance. Additional Paid-in Capital is the correct account.

1-9.q: A company writes off a customer's account balance of $2,500 as uncollectible (using the allowance method). What is the correct journal entry?
o: Debit Bad Debt Expense $2,500; Credit Accounts Receivable $2,500.
Debit Allowance for Doubtful Accounts $2,500; Credit Accounts Receivable $2,500.
Debit Accounts Receivable $2,500; Credit Allowance for Doubtful Accounts $2,500.
Debit Bad Debt Expense $2,500; Credit Allowance for Doubtful Accounts $2,500.
1-9.e: Under the allowance method, the write-off entry debits Allowance for Doubtful Accounts (reducing the previously established allowance) and credits Accounts Receivable (removing the specific uncollectible amount). Bad Debt Expense was already recorded in a prior period when the allowance was established.

wrongWhy.0: This is the direct write-off method entry, not the allowance method. Under the allowance method, the expense was already recorded when the allowance was set up.
wrongWhy.2: This entry is reversed — Accounts Receivable should be credited (reduced), not debited.
wrongWhy.3: This entry records the establishment of the allowance, not the write-off of a specific account.

1-9.q: A company exchanges old equipment (book value $8,000) for new equipment worth $15,000, paying $7,000 cash to complete the exchange. What is the gain or loss on this transaction?
o: No gain or loss.
1-9.e: The old equipment given up has a book value of $8,000. The fair value received (new equipment $15,000 minus cash paid $7,000 = $8,000 net value received). Since fair value of consideration received equals book value given up, there is no gain or loss.

wrongWhy.0: A gain arises only when the fair value received exceeds the book value given up. Here they are equal.
wrongWhy.1: A loss arises only when the book value given up exceeds the fair value received. Here they are equal.
wrongWhy.3: The values are equal — book value of old equipment ($8,000) equals net fair value received ($15,000 - $7,000 = $8,000).

1-9.q: A company receives a $10,000 bank loan. Six months later, it repays $5,000 of principal plus $300 interest. Which entry records the repayment?
o: Debit Notes Payable $5,300; Credit Cash $5,300.
Debit Notes Payable $5,000; Debit Interest Expense $300; Credit Cash $5,300.
Debit Cash $5,300; Credit Notes Payable $5,000; Credit Interest Revenue $300.
Debit Notes Payable $10,000; Credit Cash $5,300; Credit Gain on Loan $4,700.
1-9.e: The repayment has two components: principal reduction (Notes Payable $5,000 debit) and interest cost (Interest Expense $300 debit). Both reduce the cash balance (Credit Cash $5,300 total).

wrongWhy.0: Combining principal and interest into a single Notes Payable debit understates interest expense and overstates the liability reduction.
wrongWhy.2: Cash is paid out (credit), not received. Interest is an expense, not revenue.
wrongWhy.3: Only $5,000 of principal is being repaid, not the full $10,000. No gain arises from repaying a loan.

1-9.q: A company sells a building with a cost of $500,000 and accumulated depreciation of $180,000 for $340,000 cash. What is the result?
o: No gain or loss.
1-9.e: Book value = Cost - Accumulated Depreciation = $500,000 - $180,000 = $320,000. Sale price = $340,000. Since sale price ($340,000) exceeds book value ($320,000), there is a gain of $20,000.

wrongWhy.1: A loss occurs when book value exceeds sale price. Here sale price ($340,000) > book value ($320,000), so it is a gain.
wrongWhy.2: $160,000 would result from comparing sale price to original cost ignoring accumulated depreciation.
wrongWhy.3: The sale price does not equal book value ($340,000 ≠ $320,000), so there is a $20,000 gain.

1-9.q: A company converts $50,000 of accounts payable into a long-term note payable. What is the effect on the balance sheet?
o: Total liabilities decrease by $50,000.
Total liabilities increase by $50,000.
Total liabilities remain the same; current liabilities decrease and long-term liabilities increase.
Total assets decrease by $50,000 and total liabilities decrease by $50,000.
1-9.e: Converting accounts payable (current liability) to a long-term note payable (non-current liability) shifts $50,000 between liability categories. Total liabilities remain unchanged — one liability decreases as another increases by the same amount.

wrongWhy.0: Total liabilities do not decrease — the obligation is still owed, just reclassified to long-term.
wrongWhy.1: Total liabilities do not increase — the debt already existed as accounts payable.
wrongWhy.3: No cash is involved in this conversion. Total assets are not affected.

1-9.q: A company receives a $2,000 utility bill that will be paid next month. How does this affect the accounting equation?
o: Assets decrease $2,000; Equity decreases $2,000.
Liabilities increase $2,000; Equity increases $2,000.
Liabilities increase $2,000; Equity decreases $2,000.
Assets decrease $2,000; Liabilities decrease $2,000.
1-9.e: Recording an unpaid utility bill creates Utilities Expense (reduces equity through increased expenses) and Utilities Payable (a liability). Assets are unaffected because no cash has been paid. The equation balances: Liabilities +$2,000, Equity -$2,000.

wrongWhy.0: Assets are not affected — no cash has been paid. Only a liability and expense (equity) are affected.
wrongWhy.1: Equity decreases (expense is incurred), not increases.
wrongWhy.3: Assets do not decrease and liabilities do not decrease — the opposite occurs. A liability is created.

1-9.q: A company purchases land for $200,000, paying $50,000 cash and signing a mortgage for the remaining $150,000. How is this transaction classified on the statement of cash flows?
o: Operating activity outflow of $200,000.
Investing activity outflow of $200,000.
Investing activity outflow of $50,000 cash; $150,000 mortgage disclosed as a non-cash investing activity.
Financing activity outflow of $200,000.
1-9.e: Only the cash portion ($50,000) is reported as an investing activity on the cash flow statement. The mortgage portion ($150,000) is a non-cash investing and financing transaction that is disclosed in the supplemental notes to the statement of cash flows.

wrongWhy.0: Land purchases are investing activities, not operating. Also, only the cash portion is on the statement itself.
wrongWhy.1: Showing the full $200,000 as a cash outflow is incorrect — only $50,000 cash was paid. The mortgage is non-cash.
wrongWhy.3: The land purchase is an investing activity. The mortgage financing is a non-cash component disclosed separately.

1-9.q: A corporation declares a stock dividend that distributes additional shares to existing shareholders. What is the effect on total stockholders' equity?
o: Total equity increases by the market value of shares distributed.
Total equity decreases by the par value of shares distributed.
Total equity remains unchanged — amounts shift among equity accounts.
Total equity decreases by the market value of shares distributed.
1-9.e: A stock dividend redistributes amounts within stockholders' equity — it transfers amounts from Retained Earnings to Common Stock and Additional Paid-in Capital. No assets leave the company, so total equity is unchanged. This contrasts with a cash dividend, which reduces both assets and equity.

wrongWhy.0: Total equity does not increase with a stock dividend — no new value enters the company.
wrongWhy.1: Total equity does not decrease with a stock dividend — no assets are distributed.
wrongWhy.3: Unlike cash dividends, stock dividends do not reduce total equity. They simply reallocate amounts within equity accounts.

1-10.q: Which of the following is not a reporting period that would be covered by a set of interim financial statements?
o: A quarter
A half year
A fiscal year
A month
1-10.e: Interim financial statements are financial statements that cover periods of less than a fiscal year, so interim financial statements would not cover a period of a full fiscal year.

wrongWhy.0: Interim financial statements are financial statements that cover periods of less than a fiscal year, so interim financial statements could be for a quarter or for two or three quarters for the year to date during a fiscal year.
wrongWhy.1: Interim financial statements are financial statements that cover periods of less than a fiscal year, so a set of interim financial statements could be for the first half of the fiscal year.
wrongWhy.3: Interim financial statements are financial statements that cover periods of less than a fiscal year, so interim financial statements could be for a month or for several months to date during a fiscal year.

1-10.q: Which of the following is not one of the methods by which expenses are recognized?
o: Systematic and rational allocation
Immediate recognition when incurred
Cause and effect
Immediate recognition when paid in cash
1-10.e: Payment in cash is not an indicator for when an expense is recognized. Under accrual accounting, expenses are recognized when incurred as a result of transferring control of goods or services to customers and recognizing the associated revenue (cause and effect) or, if an expense will not provide future benefit, it is recognized immediately when incurred. Expenses can also be recognized on a systematic and rational allocation basis as in depreciation.

wrongWhy.0: Expenses can be recognized on a systematic and rational allocation basis, such as depreciation of fixed assets which is related to net changes in assets as they are used.
wrongWhy.1: If an expense will not provide future benefit, it is recognized immediately when it is incurred.
wrongWhy.2: Expenses are recognized when they are incurred as a result of transferring control of goods or services to customers and recognizing the associated revenue (the matching principle). The cost of an item sold is recognized as cost of goods sold expense when the sale of the item contributes to revenue.

1-10.q: A publishing company sold $7,000 in magazine subscriptions. To date, the company has received payments totaling $5,000 of the $7,000 total subscriptions. According to the accrual accounting rulings, when should revenue be recognized?
o: When the magazine is sent to subscribers.
Not until all $7,000 in subscription payments are received.
As payments are received.
When the customer confirms receipt.
1-10.e: The revenue recognition principle states that revenue is to be recognized in the accounting period in which the performance obligation to the customer is satisfied and the customer obtains control of the asset. Therefore, subscription revenues should be recognized when the magazine is sent to subscribers. When the subscription fee is paid in advance, before the magazine is sent, the amount received is called deferred revenue, and it is recorded by debiting the amount received to cash and crediting it to deferred revenue or contract liabilities. As the magazines are sent to the customers, a portion of the amount received is transferred to revenue by debiting deferred revenue/contract liabilities and crediting revenue. That is accomplished by means of adjusting entries at the end of an accounting period.

wrongWhy.1: The revenue recognition principle states that revenue is to be recognized in the accounting period in which the performance obligation to the customer is satisfied and the customer obtains control of the asset. The receipt of the cash is a separate matter from recognition of the revenue. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.2: The revenue recognition principle states that revenue is to be recognized in the accounting period in which the performance obligation to the customer is satisfied and the customer obtains control of the asset, not when cash is received. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.3: The revenue recognition principle states that revenue is to be recognized in the accounting period in which the performance obligation to the customer is satisfied and the customer obtains control of the asset. Accounting standards do not require a confirmation of receipt from the customer, although in certain circumstances, a confirmation may be desirable for internal control purposes. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-10.q: The balance sheet of Sumner Company reports retained earnings of $140,000. Total assets are $720,000 and are twice the balance of total liabilities. What is the balance of capital stock (common stock plus additional paid-in capital)?
1-10.e: If total assets are $720,000 and are twice the balance of total liabilities, total liabilities are $360,000 ($720,000 ÷ 2). Therefore, total equity is $720,000 − $360,000, or $360,000. Retained earnings is part of equity, and if retained earnings are $140,000, then capital stock must be the remainder of equity, or $360,000 − $140,000, which is $220,000.

wrongWhy.0: This is the balance of total liabilities, not the balance of capital stock. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.2: This is the balance in retained earnings, not the balance of capital stock. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.3: This is one-half of the balance in retained earnings, not the balance of capital stock. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-10.q: An Accumulated Depreciation account is
o: An expense account
A contra-asset account
A liability account
An equity account
1-10.e: An Accumulated Depreciation account is a contra-asset account. It carries a credit balance which is the opposite of the normal balance for an asset account. Thus, its balance is negative, and it serves as a valuation account that decreases the value of a fixed asset on the balance sheet.

wrongWhy.0: An Accumulated Depreciation account is not an expense account. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.2: An Accumulated Depreciation account is not a liability account. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.3: An Accumulated Depreciation account is not an equity account. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-10.q: Which of the following is not a requirement for a business transaction to be recorded in accounting records?
o: It must be something that affects the financial information presented in the organization’s financial statements
It must need to be recorded in the accounting record
It must always have supporting documentation
It must be able to be expressed in money
1-10.e: Regular daily transactions should always have supporting documentation, but adjusting entries may not always have supporting documentation.

wrongWhy.0: A transaction is a business event that can be expressed in money, that affects the financial information presented in the organization’s financial statements, and thus needs to be recorded in the accounting records.
wrongWhy.1: A transaction is a business event that can be expressed in money, that affects the financial information presented in the organization’s financial statements, and thus needs to be recorded in the accounting records.
wrongWhy.3: A transaction is a business event that can be expressed in money, that affects the financial information presented in the organization’s financial statements, and thus needs to be recorded in the accounting records.

1-10.q: XYZ Corporation pays ABC Corporation $120,000 in advance on January 1 for it to conduct a 12-month study on possible acquisitions. What is the impact on the accounting equation of this transaction on ABC’s books in February?
o: Assets, $0; liability, $(10,000); equity, $10,000.
Assets, $0; liability, $10,000; equity, $(10,000).
Assets, $0; liability, $0; equity, $0.
Assets, $(10,000); liability, $0; equity, $(10,000).
1-10.e: When the $120,000 is received on January 1, it is a liability called deferred revenue or a contract liability, and it is recorded by debiting cash for $120,000 and crediting deferred revenue/contract liabilities for $120,000. Thereafter, at the end of each month, 1/12 of that amount is debited to contract liabilities (to reduce contract liabilities) and credited to revenue (to increase revenue) as part of the period’s adjusting entries. So, at the end of February, a transaction will be recorded to debit contract liabilities for $10,000 and credit revenue for $10,000. Since revenue is a component of net income and net income flows to equity, the transaction recorded at the end of February will have the effect of decreasing liabilities by $10,000 and increasing equity by $10,000. Assets will be unaffected by the February transaction. Thus, the accounting equation, Assets = Liabilities + Equity, will remain in balance.

wrongWhy.1: When the $120,000 is received on January 1, it is recorded by debiting cash for $120,000 and crediting contract liabilities for $120,000. Thereafter, at the end of each month, 1/12 of that amount is debited to contract liabilities (to reduce contract liabilities) and credited to revenue (to increase revenue). The impact on the accounting equation in February will not be to increase liabilities by $10,000 and decrease equity by $10,000. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.2: When the $120,000 is received on January 1, it is recorded by debiting cash for $120,000 and crediting contract liabilities for $120,000. Thereafter, at the end of each month, 1/12 of that amount is debited to contract liabilities (to reduce contract liabilities) and credited to revenue (to increase revenue). The transaction recorded in February will have a non-zero impact on certain elements of the accounting equation.
wrongWhy.3: When the $120,000 is received on January 1, it is recorded by debiting cash for $120,000 and crediting contract liabilities for $120,000. Thereafter, at the end of each month, 1/12 of that amount is debited to contract liabilities (to reduce contract liabilities) and credited to revenue (to increase revenue). The impact on the accounting equation in February will not be to decrease assets by $10,000 and decrease equity by $10,000. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-10.q: Which of the following best describes the purpose of adjusting entries?
o: To correct errors made in journal entries during the period.
To update account balances so revenues and expenses are recognized in the correct period.
To transfer temporary account balances to permanent accounts.
To record transactions that were missed during the period.
1-10.e: Adjusting entries are made at period-end to ensure that revenues are recognized when earned and expenses when incurred, regardless of cash flows. They align the accounting records with the accrual basis of accounting.

wrongWhy.0: Correcting errors requires correcting entries, not adjusting entries. Adjusting entries are planned, routine entries, not corrections.
wrongWhy.2: Transferring temporary accounts to permanent accounts describes closing entries, not adjusting entries.
wrongWhy.3: Missed transactions require correcting entries. Adjusting entries address timing differences, not omissions.

1-10.q: On October 1, a company pays $6,000 for a 12-month insurance policy. What is the adjusting entry on December 31?
o: Debit Insurance Expense $6,000; Credit Prepaid Insurance $6,000.
Debit Prepaid Insurance $1,500; Credit Insurance Expense $1,500.
Debit Insurance Expense $1,500; Credit Prepaid Insurance $1,500.
Debit Insurance Expense $500; Credit Prepaid Insurance $500.
1-10.e: From October 1 to December 31 = 3 months. Monthly expense = $6,000 ÷ 12 = $500. Total expense for 3 months = $500 × 3 = $1,500. The adjusting entry recognizes the 3 months of insurance used: debit Insurance Expense $1,500, credit Prepaid Insurance $1,500.

wrongWhy.0: Expensing the full $6,000 is incorrect — only 3 of 12 months have expired by December 31.
wrongWhy.1: This entry is reversed — Prepaid Insurance should decrease (credit), not increase.
wrongWhy.3: $500 is only one month's expense. Three months have elapsed (October, November, December), so $1,500 is correct.

1-10.q: A company receives $18,000 cash in January for services to be performed evenly over 6 months (January–June). By March 31, what is the balance in Unearned Revenue?
1-10.e: Monthly revenue = $18,000 ÷ 6 = $3,000. By March 31, three months have passed (January, February, March), so $9,000 has been earned and recognized. The remaining $9,000 (for April, May, June) is still unearned: $18,000 - $9,000 = $9,000.

wrongWhy.0: $18,000 is the original balance before any services were performed. After 3 months, $9,000 has been earned.
wrongWhy.2: $6,000 would represent 2 months remaining, but 3 months have elapsed and 3 months remain.
wrongWhy.3: $3,000 represents only one month remaining, but 3 months still remain after March 31.

1-10.q: Depreciation of $3,600 for the year has not been recorded. What adjusting entry is required?
o: Debit Equipment $3,600; Credit Accumulated Depreciation $3,600.
Debit Depreciation Expense $3,600; Credit Equipment $3,600.
Debit Depreciation Expense $3,600; Credit Accumulated Depreciation $3,600.
Debit Accumulated Depreciation $3,600; Credit Equipment $3,600.
1-10.e: The adjusting entry for depreciation debits Depreciation Expense (increasing expense) and credits Accumulated Depreciation (a contra-asset account). Equipment is not directly credited — its historical cost remains in the accounts; Accumulated Depreciation shows the portion used.

wrongWhy.0: Debiting Equipment increases the asset, which is incorrect. Depreciation reduces the net book value through Accumulated Depreciation.
wrongWhy.1: Equipment is recorded at historical cost and is not directly reduced by depreciation. Accumulated Depreciation is the correct contra-asset account.
wrongWhy.3: This entry is reversed — Depreciation Expense must be debited (increased), and Accumulated Depreciation credited.

1-10.q: At year-end, employees have earned $4,200 in wages that will be paid in the next period. What adjusting entry is required?
o: Debit Salaries Payable $4,200; Credit Cash $4,200.
Debit Salaries Expense $4,200; Credit Cash $4,200.
Debit Salaries Expense $4,200; Credit Salaries Payable $4,200.
Debit Cash $4,200; Credit Salaries Expense $4,200.
1-10.e: This is an accrued expense — wages earned but not yet paid. Salaries Expense increases (debit) to recognize the cost in the current period, and Salaries Payable increases (credit) as a liability for the amount owed.

wrongWhy.0: Cash has not been paid yet. The liability is Salaries Payable — debiting it would incorrectly reduce a liability that has not yet been settled.
wrongWhy.1: Cash has not been paid. The credit should go to Salaries Payable (liability), not Cash.
wrongWhy.3: Salaries Expense increases with a debit (not credit), and no cash has been paid.

1-10.q: Which of the following is an example of a deferred revenue adjusting entry?
o: Recording interest earned but not yet received.
Recording wages incurred but not yet paid.
Recognizing revenue that was previously recorded as a liability.
Recording depreciation on long-term assets.
1-10.e: Deferred revenue adjusting entries convert previously recorded unearned revenue (a liability) to earned revenue as services are performed or goods are delivered. The entry debits Unearned Revenue and credits Revenue.

wrongWhy.0: Recording interest earned but not received is an accrued revenue entry, not a deferred revenue entry.
wrongWhy.1: Recording wages incurred but not paid is an accrued expense entry.
wrongWhy.3: Depreciation is an allocation of asset cost — it is an adjusting entry but not related to deferred revenue.

1-10.q: A company earns $800 of interest on a note receivable by December 31 but will not receive the cash until next year. What adjusting entry is required?
o: Debit Interest Revenue $800; Credit Interest Receivable $800.
Debit Cash $800; Credit Interest Revenue $800.
Debit Interest Receivable $800; Credit Interest Revenue $800.
Debit Interest Expense $800; Credit Interest Payable $800.
1-10.e: This is an accrued revenue entry. Interest Receivable (an asset) increases to reflect the right to receive payment, and Interest Revenue increases to recognize the income earned in the current period.

wrongWhy.0: This is reversed — Interest Receivable should be debited (asset increasing) and Interest Revenue credited.
wrongWhy.1: Cash has not been received yet. Interest Receivable should be debited.
wrongWhy.3: Interest Expense and Interest Payable are used when a company owes interest, not when it has earned interest.

1-10.q: If adjusting entries are not recorded at period-end, which of the following will occur?
o: Net income will always be overstated.
The trial balance will not balance.
Revenues and expenses will be mismatched across periods, distorting net income.
Cash balances will be incorrect.
1-10.e: Failing to record adjusting entries causes revenues and expenses to be recognized in the wrong periods, violating the matching principle. This distorts net income — it may be overstated, understated, or misstated depending on the specific adjustments missed.

wrongWhy.0: Net income could be overstated or understated depending on which adjusting entries are omitted. It is not always overstated.
wrongWhy.1: The trial balance may still balance even without adjusting entries — the imbalance is between the correct economic activity and what is recorded, not necessarily in debits vs. credits.
wrongWhy.3: Cash balances are not affected by adjusting entries — adjusting entries typically do not involve cash.

1-10.q: The Supplies account shows a debit balance of $5,500 before adjustment. A physical count reveals $1,800 of supplies on hand. What is the adjusting entry?
o: Debit Supplies Expense $1,800; Credit Supplies $1,800.
Debit Supplies $3,700; Credit Supplies Expense $3,700.
Debit Supplies Expense $3,700; Credit Supplies $3,700.
Debit Supplies Expense $5,500; Credit Supplies $5,500.
1-10.e: Supplies used = $5,500 - $1,800 = $3,700. The adjusting entry records the $3,700 used as Supplies Expense (debit) and reduces the Supplies asset by $3,700 (credit). After the entry, Supplies will have a balance of $1,800.

wrongWhy.0: Only $1,800 is on hand — this is the remaining balance, not the amount used. The amount used (expensed) is $3,700.
wrongWhy.1: This entry would increase Supplies and decrease expense, which is the reverse of what is needed.
wrongWhy.3: Expensing the full $5,500 would leave a zero balance in Supplies, but $1,800 of supplies still remains on hand.

1-10.q: Which type of adjusting entry is made when a company has incurred an expense that will be paid in the next accounting period?
o: Deferred expense adjustment.
Deferred revenue adjustment.
Accrued expense adjustment.
Accrued revenue adjustment.
1-10.e: An accrued expense adjustment records an expense that has been incurred but not yet paid. It debits an expense account and credits a liability account (such as Wages Payable or Interest Payable), recognizing the cost in the current period.

wrongWhy.0: A deferred expense adjustment converts a prepaid asset to expense over time. It applies when cash was paid in advance, not when payment comes later.
wrongWhy.1: A deferred revenue adjustment converts unearned revenue to earned revenue. It involves revenue, not expenses.
wrongWhy.3: An accrued revenue adjustment records revenue earned but not yet received. It involves revenue that has been earned, not expenses incurred.

1-10.q: A company rents out office space and receives $24,000 on November 1 for 12 months of rent. On December 31, how much should be in the Unearned Rent Revenue account?
1-10.e: Monthly rent = $24,000 ÷ 12 = $2,000. Two months have passed (November and December), so $4,000 has been earned and recognized as revenue. Unearned Rent Revenue = $24,000 - $4,000 = $20,000 still remaining as a liability.

wrongWhy.0: $24,000 is the original balance before any revenue was earned. After 2 months, $4,000 has been earned.
wrongWhy.1: $4,000 is the amount earned (recognized as revenue) for November and December, not the unearned balance.
wrongWhy.3: $2,000 is one month's rent — only one month, not the two months that have elapsed.

1-10.q: After recording all adjusting entries, which document is prepared next?
o: Unadjusted trial balance.
Adjusted trial balance.
Post-closing trial balance.
Statement of cash flows.
1-10.e: After adjusting entries are posted, an adjusted trial balance is prepared to verify that total debits still equal total credits. The adjusted trial balance is then used to prepare the financial statements.

wrongWhy.0: The unadjusted trial balance is prepared before adjusting entries. It precedes, not follows, the adjusting entries.
wrongWhy.2: The post-closing trial balance is prepared after closing entries at the very end of the cycle.
wrongWhy.3: Financial statements, including the cash flow statement, are prepared after the adjusted trial balance — not immediately after adjusting entries.

1-10.q: Which of the following adjusting entries would increase net income?
o: Recording wages earned but unpaid at period-end.
Recording depreciation on equipment.
Recognizing revenue that was previously recorded as unearned.
Writing off supplies used during the period.
1-10.e: Recognizing earned revenue (debit Unearned Revenue, credit Revenue) increases revenue and therefore increases net income. The other options all increase expenses, which decreases net income.

wrongWhy.0: Recording unpaid wages increases Salaries Expense, which decreases net income.
wrongWhy.1: Recording depreciation increases Depreciation Expense, which decreases net income.
wrongWhy.3: Writing off supplies used increases Supplies Expense, which decreases net income.

1-11.q: How are fixed assets accounted for?
o: They are initially recorded as assets on the balance sheet at their historical cost, and subsequently their values are decreased as they wear out by decreasing their historical cost.
They are expensed as incurred.
They are initially recorded as assets on the balance sheet at their historical cost, and subsequently their value is decreased whenever they lose market value.

They are initially recorded as assets on the balance sheet at their historical cost, and subsequently the book values of those that are depreciable are decreased by means of a valuation account, Accumulated Depreciation.
1-11.e: Fixed assets are initially recorded as assets on the balance sheet at their historical cost. Subsequently, the book values of all fixed assets other than land are decreased by recording depreciation on them. The depreciation is recorded by debiting the appropriate depreciation account and crediting Accumulated Depreciation, a valuation account. The Accumulated Depreciation account follows its related fixed asset account in the Chart of Accounts and carries a credit balance. When the credit balance in the Accumulated Depreciation account and the historical cost of the fixed asset in the related fixed asset account are combined, the result is the book value of the fixed asset. The book value of the fixed asset declines as the depreciation on the asset is recorded.

wrongWhy.0: Fixed assets are initially recorded as assets on the balance sheet at their historical cost. However, their values are not decreased as they wear out by decreasing their historical cost. The historical cost recorded does not change. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.1: Fixed assets are not expensed as incurred. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.2: Fixed assets are initially recorded as assets on the balance sheet at their historical cost. However, their value is not subsequently decreased whenever they lose market value. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-11.q: The combined balance of a fixed asset account and its related accumulated depreciation account is the asset’s
o: Replacement value
Liquidation value
Book value
Market value
1-11.e: The combined balance of a fixed asset account and its related accumulated depreciation account is the asset’s book value, also called its carrying value. The accumulated depreciation account is a valuation account that carries a credit (negative) balance and, when combined with the related asset’s historical cost in the fixed asset account, reduces the value of the related asset as reported on the balance sheet.

wrongWhy.0: The combined balance of a fixed asset account and its related accumulated depreciation account is not the asset’s replacement value. The replacement value of an asset is independent of the value accorded to the asset in the company’s balance sheet. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.1: The combined balance of a fixed asset account and its related accumulated depreciation account is not the asset’s liquidation value. An asset’s liquidation value is the value at which it should be reported on the balance sheet if the company is no longer a going concern and liquidation is imminent.
wrongWhy.3: The combined balance of a fixed asset account and its related accumulated depreciation account is not the asset’s market value. The market value of an asset is independent of the value accorded to the asset in the company’s balance sheet. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-11.q: What is the relationship between the adjusted trial balance and the financial statements?
o: The adjusted trial balance is used as the basis for preparing the financial statements.
All asset account balances are in the left column of the adjusted trial balance and all liability and equity account balances are in the right column of the adjusted trial balance.
The financial statements can be used to make corrections to the adjusted trial balance if the adjusted trial balance does not balance.
The total debits and total credits on the adjusted trial balance should be equal to total assets and total liabilities plus equity on the balance sheet.
1-11.e: Financial statements are prepared from the adjusted trial balance.

wrongWhy.1: All debit account balances are in the left column of the adjusted trial balance and all credit account balances are in the right column of the adjusted trial balance.
wrongWhy.2: The financial statements cannot be used to make corrections to the adjusted trial balance if the adjusted trial balance does not balance. If the adjusted trial balance does not balance, the error or errors must be investigated and corrected before it can be used to prepare the financial statements.
wrongWhy.3: The total debits and total credits on the adjusted trial balance should not be equal to total assets and total liabilities plus equity on the balance sheet.

1-11.q: After the period-end adjusting entries have been recorded in the accounting system, the next step in the accounting cycle is to
o: prepare an adjusted trial balance.
reverse the accruals.
prepare financial statements.
perform the year-end close.
1-11.e: After the period-end adjusting entries have been recorded, the next step is to prepare an adjusted trial balance that includes all the adjusting entries.

wrongWhy.1: After the period-end adjusting entries have been recorded, reversing the accruals is not the next step in the accounting cycle. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
