wrongWhy.2: After the period-end adjusting entries have been recorded, preparation of the financial statements is not the next step in the accounting cycle. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.3: After the period-end adjusting entries have been recorded, performing the year-end close is not the next step in the accounting cycle. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-11.q: Over how many years should the cost of the land on which a building is constructed be depreciated?
o: Thirty years.
The estimated life of the building.
None.
Fifteen years.
1-11.e: Land is never depreciated because it does not wear out or get used up, even if a building has been constructed on it. Therefore, the historical cost of property must be allocated between the cost of the land and the cost of the building on the land. The cost of the building on the land is depreciated over the building’s estimated useful life, while the cost of the land is not depreciated.

wrongWhy.0: The land on which a building is constructed is not depreciated over thirty years. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.1: The land on which a building is constructed is not depreciated over the estimated life of the building. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.3: The land on which a building is constructed is not depreciated over fifteen years. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-11.q: DFI Inc. rents a warehouse for which it pays rent of $120,000 per year, payable in advance. On January 1, 20X4, DFI makes its 20X4 rent payment in cash. DFI issues financial statements twice a year: interim financial statements on June 30 and the annual financial statements at the end of its fiscal year on December 31. What adjusting entry should be made on June 30, 20X4 for the rent?
o: Debit Rent Expense for $60,000 and credit Prepaid Expense for $60,000.
Debit Prepaid Expense for $120,000 and credit Rent Expense for $120,000.
Debit Rent Expense for $120,000 and credit Prepaid Expense for $120,000.
Debit Prepaid Expense for $60,000 and credit Rent Expense for $60,000.
1-11.e: The $120,000 for one year’s rent was paid in advance on January 1. The $120,000 payment on January 1 was debited to an asset account, Prepaid Expense, and credited to Cash. As of June 30, one-half of the annual rent has been used. Therefore, one-half of the amount paid, or $60,000, should be debited to Rent Expense (to increase rent expense) and credited to Prepaid Expense (to decrease the Prepaid Expense asset’s balance) to the amount of benefit from the advance payment that is still to be received as of June 30.

wrongWhy.1: This entry is backwards, and the amount is too great. A debit to an asset account increases the asset’s balance, and Prepaid Expense, an asset account, needs to be decreased instead of increased. A credit to an expense account decreases the expense, and Rent Expense needs to be increased instead of decreased. Furthermore, the transaction should be for only one-half of the rent payment made on January 1 because only one-half of the year has passed and only one-half of the rent paid in advance has been used.
wrongWhy.2: As of June 30, only one-half of the annual rent amount paid in advance has been used. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.3: This entry is backwards. A debit to an asset account increases the asset’s balance, and Prepaid Expense, an asset account, needs to be decreased instead of increased. A credit to an expense account decreases the expense, and Rent Expense needs to be increased instead of decreased. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-11.q: On June 15, Camping Supply, a retail store, borrowed $50,000 on a short-term note at an annual interest rate of 6.935% to purchase inventory. The principal plus accrued interest is due in 90 days, on September 13. Camping Supply publishes monthly financial statements and accrues the interest expense monthly. Using a 365-day year, how much interest expense should Camping Supply accrue on August 31?
1-11.e: On August 31, Camping Supply will accrue interest expense for 31 days, from August 1 to August 31. Using a 365-day year, 31 days of interest on $50,000 at an annual interest rate of 6.935% is calculated as $50,000 × 0.06935 ÷ 365 × 31 = $294.50.

wrongWhy.0: This is the amount of interest due for the 16 days in June that the loan was outstanding, from June 15 to June 30. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.2: This is the interest that will be payable on September 13, the maturity date of the note, for the full 90 days the loan will be outstanding. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.3: This is the total interest expense accrued from the date the loan was disbursed on June 15 to August 31 (78 days). However, Camping Supply accrues the interest due monthly, so the interest accrued on August 31 will be only the interest expense for the month of August. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-11.q: At the end of a reporting period, estimating costs incurred through the closing date of the period and recording them in the accounting system if invoices have not been received
o: is called deferring the expenses and is a necessary part of the accounting cycle.
is called accruing the expenses and is a necessary part of the accounting cycle.
may be done at management’s discretion if the expenses are material.
may not be done because expenses cannot be recorded until the invoices are received.
1-11.e: According to generally accepted accounting principles, which require accrual accounting, estimating costs incurred through the closing date of the period and recording them in the accounting system is required, even if invoices have not yet been received. The estimation and recording of the expenses is called accruing the expenses, and it is a necessary part of the accounting cycle.

wrongWhy.0: Estimating costs incurred through the closing date of the period and recording them in the accounting system, even if invoices have not been received, is not called deferring the expenses. A deferred expense occurs when a company makes a payment in advance of receiving a good or a service, and the transaction is reported as an asset on the balance sheet until the payor receives the benefit from the payment. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.2: According to generally accepted accounting principles, which require accrual accounting, estimating costs incurred through the closing date of the period and recording them in the accounting system is required. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.3: According to generally accepted accounting principles, which require accrual accounting, estimating costs incurred through the closing date of the period and recording them in the accounting system is required. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-11.q: At the end of the fiscal year, Hub Corporation records depreciation on its fixed assets according to the depreciation schedules developed when the assets were purchased. How are the depreciation amounts recorded in the accounting system?
o: Debit Depreciation Expense, credit Cash
Debit Accumulated Depreciation, credit Depreciation Expense
Debit Depreciation Expense, credit Accumulated Depreciation
Debit Depreciation Expense, credit Fixed Assets
1-11.e: Depreciation is usually recorded by debiting an expense account, Depreciation Expense, and crediting the contra-asset account Accumulated Depreciation.

wrongWhy.0: Depreciation is a non-cash transaction. It does not involve the payment of cash. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.1: This is backwards. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.3: The credit portion of the transaction is not a credit to the Fixed Assets account. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-11.q: On December 31, a company earned $800 in interest on a note receivable but has not yet received cash. What adjusting entry is required?
o: Debit: Cash $800; Credit: Interest Revenue $800.
Debit: Interest Receivable $800; Credit: Interest Revenue $800.
Debit: Interest Revenue $800; Credit: Interest Receivable $800.
Debit: Notes Receivable $800; Credit: Interest Revenue $800.
1-11.e: Interest Receivable (asset) is debited to record the amount owed to the company, and Interest Revenue is credited to recognize revenue earned. This is an accrued revenue entry.

wrongWhy.0: Cash has not been received yet — this entry is only made when cash is actually collected.
wrongWhy.2: Reversed — it would decrease Interest Revenue and increase Interest Receivable incorrectly.
wrongWhy.3: Notes Receivable represents the loan principal, not interest earned. Interest Receivable is the correct account.

1-11.q: A company paid $6,000 for rent on November 1 covering 6 months. On December 31, what adjusting entry is needed?
o: Debit: Prepaid Rent $2,000; Credit: Rent Expense $2,000.
Debit: Rent Expense $6,000; Credit: Prepaid Rent $6,000.
Debit: Rent Expense $2,000; Credit: Prepaid Rent $2,000.
Debit: Prepaid Rent $6,000; Credit: Cash $6,000.
1-11.e: Two months used by December 31: 2/6 × $6,000 = $2,000. Rent Expense is debited and Prepaid Rent is credited to reduce the asset.

wrongWhy.0: Reversed — it would increase Prepaid Rent (asset) and decrease Rent Expense incorrectly.
wrongWhy.1: Only 2 of 6 months have been used. Expensing the full $6,000 overstates expenses.
wrongWhy.3: This records the initial cash payment, not an adjusting entry.

1-11.q: Which of the following is an example of an accrued expense?
o: Rent paid in advance for the next quarter.
Depreciation of equipment recorded at year-end.
Salaries earned by employees but not yet paid at month-end.
Revenue collected before services are performed.
1-11.e: An accrued expense is incurred but not yet paid. Salaries earned but unpaid must be recorded as Salaries Expense (debit) and Salaries Payable (credit).

wrongWhy.0: Rent paid in advance is a prepaid expense — an asset representing a future benefit already paid for.
wrongWhy.1: Depreciation is a cost allocation, not an accrued expense.
wrongWhy.3: Revenue collected before services are performed is unearned revenue — a liability.

1-11.q: At year-end, $1,500 of supplies have been used. The Supplies account had a balance of $4,000. What is the adjusting entry?
o: Debit: Supplies $1,500; Credit: Supplies Expense $1,500.
Debit: Supplies Expense $4,000; Credit: Supplies $4,000.
Debit: Supplies Expense $1,500; Credit: Supplies $1,500.
Debit: Supplies $2,500; Credit: Cash $2,500.
1-11.e: The $1,500 consumed is expensed: Supplies Expense is debited and Supplies is credited. Remaining balance = $4,000 - $1,500 = $2,500 of unused supplies on hand.

wrongWhy.0: Reversed — increases Supplies asset and decreases Supplies Expense incorrectly.
wrongWhy.1: Only $1,500 was used, not $4,000. Expensing the full balance eliminates the asset and overstates expenses.
wrongWhy.3: No cash transaction is involved in a supplies adjusting entry.

1-11.q: A company borrowed $50,000 on December 1 at 6% annual interest. What adjusting entry is needed on December 31?
o: Debit: Interest Expense $3,000; Credit: Interest Payable $3,000.
Debit: Interest Expense $250; Credit: Interest Payable $250.
Debit: Interest Payable $250; Credit: Interest Expense $250.
Debit: Notes Payable $250; Credit: Cash $250.
1-11.e: One month of interest: $50,000 × 6% × 1/12 = $250. Interest Expense is debited to recognize the cost and Interest Payable is credited to record the liability.

wrongWhy.0: $3,000 is the full annual interest. The adjusting entry is for one month only: $250.
wrongWhy.2: Reversed — would decrease Interest Payable and Interest Expense incorrectly.
wrongWhy.3: No cash has been paid yet. This is an accrual entry.

1-11.q: What type of adjusting entry converts a liability into revenue?
o: Accrued revenue adjustment.
Prepaid expense adjustment.
Unearned revenue adjustment.
Accrued expense adjustment.
1-11.e: Cash received in advance is recorded as Unearned Revenue (liability). As services are performed, the entry debits Unearned Revenue (reducing the liability) and credits Revenue (recognizing income earned).

wrongWhy.0: Accrued revenue records revenue earned but not yet received — creates an asset, not reduces a liability.
wrongWhy.1: Prepaid expense adjustment converts an asset to an expense — no liability-to-revenue conversion.
wrongWhy.3: Accrued expense adjustment records an expense not yet paid — creates a liability.

1-11.q: Equipment costing $24,000 with an 8-year useful life and no salvage value. What is the monthly straight-line depreciation adjusting entry?
o: Debit: Depreciation Expense $3,000; Credit: Accumulated Depreciation $3,000.
Debit: Equipment $250; Credit: Depreciation Expense $250.
Debit: Depreciation Expense $250; Credit: Accumulated Depreciation $250.
Debit: Depreciation Expense $250; Credit: Equipment $250.
1-11.e: Annual depreciation = $24,000 / 8 = $3,000. Monthly = $3,000 / 12 = $250. Depreciation Expense is debited and Accumulated Depreciation (contra-asset) is credited, preserving the Equipment account at cost.

wrongWhy.0: $3,000 is the annual amount, not monthly. The monthly entry is for $250.
wrongWhy.1: Incorrect direction — Equipment should not be debited and Depreciation Expense should not be credited.
wrongWhy.3: Equipment should not be directly credited. Accumulated Depreciation is the correct contra-asset account.

1-11.q: Unearned Service Revenue shows $9,000 before adjustment. $6,000 of services have been performed by year-end. What adjusting entry is required?
o: Debit: Service Revenue $6,000; Credit: Unearned Service Revenue $6,000.
Debit: Unearned Service Revenue $9,000; Credit: Service Revenue $9,000.
Debit: Unearned Service Revenue $6,000; Credit: Service Revenue $6,000.
Debit: Cash $6,000; Credit: Service Revenue $6,000.
1-11.e: Unearned Service Revenue is debited by $6,000 (reducing liability) and Service Revenue is credited by $6,000 (recognizing income). The remaining $3,000 stays as a liability.

wrongWhy.0: Service Revenue should be credited, not debited.
wrongWhy.1: Only $6,000 has been earned. Recognizing $9,000 overstates revenue.
wrongWhy.3: No cash changes hands in this adjusting entry.

1-11.q: Which statement about adjusting entries is correct?
o: Adjusting entries always involve cash.
Adjusting entries are made at the beginning of the accounting period.
Adjusting entries update accounts before financial statements are prepared.
Adjusting entries are only needed when errors have been made.
1-11.e: Adjusting entries are made at the end of each accounting period to ensure revenues are recognized when earned and expenses when incurred, per accrual accounting.

wrongWhy.0: Adjusting entries never involve cash — if cash is part of a transaction, it is a regular journal entry.
wrongWhy.1: Adjusting entries are made at the END of the period, not the beginning.
wrongWhy.3: Adjusting entries are a normal part of the accounting cycle, not error corrections.

1-11.q: A company signed a 3-month consulting contract for $12,000 starting November 1. By December 31, two months of work are complete but no cash has been received. What adjusting entry is needed?
o: Debit: Accounts Receivable $12,000; Credit: Consulting Revenue $12,000.
Debit: Accounts Receivable $8,000; Credit: Consulting Revenue $8,000.
Debit: Cash $8,000; Credit: Consulting Revenue $8,000.
Debit: Consulting Revenue $8,000; Credit: Unearned Revenue $8,000.
1-11.e: Two of three months completed: 2/3 × $12,000 = $8,000 earned. Accounts Receivable is debited (amount owed to company) and Consulting Revenue is credited (revenue earned).

wrongWhy.0: Only 2 of 3 months are complete. The full $12,000 has not been earned yet.
wrongWhy.2: No cash received. Debiting Cash is incorrect — Accounts Receivable should be debited.
wrongWhy.3: Unearned Revenue is used when cash is received before services. Here, the company is owed money — it has earned revenue but not collected it.

1-11.q: After adjusting entries are posted, which account would appear on the adjusted trial balance but NOT on the unadjusted trial balance?
o: Cash.
Accounts Payable.
Interest Payable.
Common Stock.
1-11.e: Interest Payable is an accrued liability created by an adjusting entry. It does not exist before the adjustment, so it only appears on the adjusted trial balance.

wrongWhy.0: Cash is updated through regular transactions and appears on both trial balances.
wrongWhy.1: Accounts Payable is created through normal purchasing transactions and appears on both trial balances.
wrongWhy.3: Common Stock is updated when shares are issued and appears on both trial balances.

1-12.q: On the post-closing trial balance, the Dividends account will have a balance
o: equal to the total dividends declared during the year.
equal to net income on the income statement.
equal to total dividends paid during the year.
equal to zero.
1-12.e: The balance in the Dividends ledger account, a contra-equity account, on the post-closing trial balance will be zero. On the date a dividend is declared, the Dividends account is debited and Dividends Payable, a liability account, is credited for the amount of the dividend. When the dividend is paid at a later date, Dividends Payable is debited and Cash is credited; the Dividends account’s balance is unchanged by the payment of the dividend and remains until the year-end close is performed. When the Dividends account is closed in the year-end close, the debit balance in the account is credited to reduce the account balance to zero and Retained Earnings is debited to reduce it by the amount of the dividends declared during the year.

wrongWhy.0: The balance in the Dividends account before the year-end closing entries are posted will be equal to the total dividends declared during the year; but that will not be true on the post-closing trial balance. The post-closing balance of the Dividends account is the balance in the account after the closing entries have been posted. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.1: The balance in the Dividends account on the post-closing trial balance will not be equal to net income on the income statement. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.2: The balance in the Dividends account on the post-closing trial balance will not be equal to total dividends paid during the year. The payment of a dividend affects only the Cash (asset) account and the Dividends Payable (liability) account, and assets and liabilities are unchanged in the yearend closing process. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-12.q: What happens to a temporary ledger account in the year-end close?
o: It is marked as closed, and its ending balance becomes its beginning balance for the next year.
Its beginning balance for the current year is reset to zero.
Its balance is reset to zero as of the closing date.
The account is automatically removed from the Chart of Accounts.
1-12.e: Temporary ledger accounts accumulate their balances during a reporting period. At the end of the reporting period they are closed and their balances are transferred to Retained Earnings, a permanent account in the Equity section of the balance sheet. Closing a temporary ledger account resets its balance to zero as of the closing date in preparation for the next year’s activity.

wrongWhy.0: Temporary accounts are not marked as closed, and their ending balances do not become their beginning balances for the next year. A ledger account that keeps increasing and decreasing from one year to the next and whose ending balance becomes its beginning balance for the next year is a permanent account. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.1: Closing a temporary ledger account does reset its balance to zero, but the effective date of the reset is not the beginning of the reporting period. Its balance is reset to zero as of the closing date, which is the end of the reporting period. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.3: Closing a temporary ledger account does not remove it from the Chart of Accounts. The account remains in the Chart of Accounts for use in the next reporting period. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-12.q: What happens to a permanent ledger account in the year-end close?
o: The Dividends account is the only permanent account whose balance changes in the year-end close, and the other permanent account balances do not change.
The Retained Earnings account is the only permanent account whose balance changes in the year-end close, and the other permanent account balances do not change.
All permanent accounts are marked as closed and their balances are reset to zero as of the closing date.
The balances of two permanent accounts, the Retained Earnings account and the Dividends account, are changed in the year-end close, and the other permanent account balances do not change.
1-12.e: The Retained Earnings account is the only permanent ledger account whose balance is changed in the year-end close. The Retained Earnings account is a permanent account in the Equity section of the balance sheet. The Dividends account is a contra-equity account in the Equity section of the balance sheet and its balance does change in the year-end close; but unlike other balance sheet accounts, it is a temporary, not a permanent, ledger account. In the year-end close, the balances in all the temporary accounts—the income statement accounts and the Dividends account—are transferred to the Retained Earnings account so the temporary accounts can start the new year with zero balances. Debit balances in temporary accounts reduce Retained Earnings, and credit balances in temporary accounts increase Retained Earnings. The other balance sheet accounts (other than Dividends) are not closed out and their beginning balances for the next year are the same as their ending balances for the current year. Note: A company may choose not to have a separate Dividends account and may instead debit dividends directly to the Retained Earnings account when they are declared.

wrongWhy.2: Permanent accounts are not closed, they are not marked as closed, and their balances are not reset to zero as of the closing date. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.3: The balances of the Retained Earnings account and the Dividends account do both change in the year-end close. However, of those two accounts only the Retained Earnings account is a permanent account. The Dividends account is a temporary account, even though it is in the Equity section of the balance sheet. The Dividends account is where the amount of dividends declared are recorded as debits, and it functions as a contra-equity account because it carries a debit balance. In the year-end close, the balances in all the temporary ledger accounts—the income statement accounts and the Dividends account—are transferred to the Retained Earnings account. Debit balances in temporary ledger accounts reduce Retained Earnings, and credit balances in temporary ledger accounts increase Retained Earnings. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-12.q: On the post-closing trial balance, the Income Summary account will have a balance
o: Equal to total dividends declared during the year.
Equal to net income for the year.
Equal to net income less dividends declared during the year.
equal to zero.
1-12.e: The balance in the Income Summary account on the post-closing trial balance will be zero. The Income Summary account serves as a control account when a manual accounting information system is being used. Temporary income statement accounts are closed to the Income Summary account, and the resulting balance in the Income Summary account should be equal to the net income or net loss on the income statement for the year. When that reconciliation is complete, the balance in the Income Summary account is closed to Retained Earnings, leaving the Income Summary account with a balance of zero on the post-closing trial balance.

wrongWhy.0: The balance in the Income Summary account on the post-closing trial balance will not be equal to total dividends declared during the year. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.1: The balance in the Income Summary account on the post-closing trial balance will not be equal to net income for the year. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.2: The balance in the Income Summary account on the post-closing trial balance will not be equal to net income less dividends declared during the year. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-12.q: The steps in the year-end closing process, in the correct order, are
o: Close the income statement accounts to the Income Summary account; close the Dividends account to the Income Summary account; close the Income Summary account to Retained Earnings
Close the income statement accounts to the Dividends account; close the Dividends account to Retained Earnings
Close the income statement accounts to the Income Summary account; close the Income Summary account to Retained Earnings; close the Dividends account to Retained Earnings
Close the Dividends account to Retained Earnings; Close the Income Summary account to Retained Earnings; Close the income statement accounts to Income Summary
1-12.e: This is the correct order of the steps in the year-end closing process. 1. Close the income statement accounts to the Income Summary account by debiting accounts with credit balances and recording the offsetting credit in Income Summary; and crediting accounts with debit balances and recording the offsetting debit in Income Summary. Compare the resulting balance in the Income Summary account with net income (loss) on the income statement to make sure they match. 2. If the Income Summary matches net income (loss), close the Income Summary account balance Retained Earnings by debiting a credit balance in Income Summary or crediting a debit balance in Income Summary and recording the offsetting credit or debit in Retained Earnings. 3. Close the Dividends account separately to Retained Earnings by debiting Retained Earnings and crediting Dividends. The result of the closing process is that year-end Retained Earnings increases (decreases) by the amount of the net income (net loss) for the period and decreases by the amount of the dividends declared during the period.

wrongWhy.0: These are not the correct steps in the year-end closing process. The Dividends account is not closed to the Income Summary account. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.1: These are not the correct steps in the year-end closing process. The income statement accounts are not closed to the Dividends account. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.3: This is the reverse of the correct order for the steps in the year-end closing process. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-12.q: How is the Income Summary account classified in the Chart of Accounts?
o: As an Asset account
As an Income account
As an Equity account
None of the above
1-12.e: The Income Summary account is not an asset, liability, equity, or income statement (revenue, gain, expense, or loss) account. It is an unclassified, temporary holding account in the Chart of Accounts that is used to provide a check on the balance reported for the net income or net loss on the income statement. Therefore, it is an internal control used in the closing process that is primarily used with manual accounting systems.

wrongWhy.0: The Income Summary account is not classified as an Asset account in the Chart of Accounts. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.1: There is no account classification in the Chart of Accounts called “Income.” Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.2: The Income Summary account is not classified as an Equity account in the Chart of Accounts. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-12.q: The purpose of the year-end close is to
o: transfer the balances in the temporary ledger accounts to the retained earnings account.
transfer the balances in the temporary ledger accounts to the net income account.
transfer the balances in the subsidiary ledger accounts to their related control accounts.
reset the balance in the retained earnings ledger account to zero.
1-12.e: The purpose of the year-end close is to transfer the balances in the temporary ledger accounts— the income statement accounts and the dividends account—to retained earnings, a permanent ledger account, and to reset the balances in the temporary accounts to zero to start the new year.

wrongWhy.1: The purpose of the year-end close is not to transfer the balances in the temporary ledger accounts to the net income account. There is no net income account. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.2: The purpose of the year-end close is not to transfer the balances in the subsidiary ledger accounts to their related control accounts. Subsidiary ledger accounts contain the detail for control accounts, and the sum of the balances in each control account’s sub-ledger accounts should equal the balance in the control account. The subsidiary ledger balances are never transferred to their control account because their balances are already included in the control account balance. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.3: The purpose of the year-end close is not to reset the balance in the retained earnings account to zero. The retained earnings account is a permanent account, and permanent accounts are not reset to zero. Their balances keep increasing and decreasing, and their beginning balances each year are equal to their ending balances at the end of the previous year. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-12.q: What is the primary purpose of closing entries?
o: To correct errors found in the adjusted trial balance.
To transfer temporary account balances to permanent accounts and prepare for the next period.
To update asset and liability balances for period-end adjustments.
To prepare the statement of cash flows.
1-12.e: Closing entries zero out temporary accounts (revenues, expenses, and dividends) by transferring their balances to Retained Earnings, a permanent account. This resets the temporary accounts to zero for the new period.

wrongWhy.0: Correcting errors requires correcting entries, not closing entries. Closing entries are a planned routine process.
wrongWhy.2: Updating assets and liabilities for period-end is done through adjusting entries, not closing entries.
wrongWhy.3: The statement of cash flows is prepared from financial statement data. Closing entries do not prepare it.

1-12.q: Which of the following accounts is closed at year-end?
o: Accounts Payable.
Retained Earnings.
Accumulated Depreciation.
Salaries Expense.
1-12.e: Salaries Expense is a temporary account (income statement account) that is closed to zero at year-end by crediting it and debiting Income Summary. Accounts Payable, Retained Earnings, and Accumulated Depreciation are permanent accounts that carry their balances into the next period.

wrongWhy.0: Accounts Payable is a permanent liability account — its balance carries forward to the next period.
wrongWhy.1: Retained Earnings is a permanent equity account — it receives the net income from closing entries. It is not itself closed.
wrongWhy.2: Accumulated Depreciation is a permanent contra-asset account whose balance accumulates over the asset's life.

1-12.q: When closing revenue accounts, which of the following entries is correct?
o: Debit Revenue; Credit Retained Earnings.
Debit Revenue; Credit Income Summary.
Debit Income Summary; Credit Revenue.
Debit Retained Earnings; Credit Revenue.
1-12.e: The first closing entry debits each revenue account (to zero it out) and credits Income Summary. Revenue accounts normally have credit balances, so they are debited to close them. The total flows into Income Summary.

wrongWhy.0: Revenues are first closed to Income Summary, not directly to Retained Earnings.
wrongWhy.2: This would credit Revenue, increasing it rather than closing it. Revenue is debited to close it.
wrongWhy.3: Debiting Retained Earnings would reduce equity, which is the opposite of what closing revenues should do.

1-12.q: What is the Income Summary account used for?
o: To record revenues and expenses throughout the period as an alternative to the income statement.
To temporarily accumulate revenues and expenses during the closing process before transferring to Retained Earnings.
To record the company's net income on the balance sheet.
To report comprehensive income including OCI items.
1-12.e: Income Summary is a temporary account used only during the closing process. Revenues are closed into it, then expenses are closed out of it, leaving a balance equal to net income (or net loss), which is then transferred to Retained Earnings.

wrongWhy.0: Revenues and expenses are recorded in their own accounts throughout the period. Income Summary is used only during the closing process.
wrongWhy.2: Net income appears on the income statement and increases Retained Earnings on the balance sheet. Income Summary is closed out and has no balance on the balance sheet.
wrongWhy.3: OCI items are reported in the Statement of Comprehensive Income, not through Income Summary.

1-12.q: A company has total revenues of $180,000 and total expenses of $135,000. What entry closes Income Summary to Retained Earnings?
o: Debit Income Summary $180,000; Credit Retained Earnings $180,000.
Debit Retained Earnings $135,000; Credit Income Summary $135,000.
Debit Income Summary $45,000; Credit Retained Earnings $45,000.
Debit Retained Earnings $45,000; Credit Income Summary $45,000.
1-12.e: Net income = $180,000 - $135,000 = $45,000. Income Summary has a credit balance of $45,000 (revenues > expenses). The closing entry debits Income Summary $45,000 to zero it out and credits Retained Earnings $45,000 to increase equity by the net income.

wrongWhy.0: Only the net income amount ($45,000) is transferred to Retained Earnings, not the full revenue amount.
wrongWhy.1: Only the net income amount is transferred, not total expenses. Also, this would reduce Retained Earnings instead of increasing it.
wrongWhy.3: When revenues exceed expenses, Income Summary has a credit balance and net income increases equity. Retained Earnings is credited (increased), not debited.

1-12.q: After all closing entries are posted, which accounts will have a zero balance?
o: All asset and liability accounts.
All revenue, expense, and dividend accounts.
Only expense accounts.
All accounts except cash.
1-12.e: Closing entries zero out all temporary accounts: revenues, expenses, and dividends (or owner's drawings). These accounts start fresh each new period. Permanent accounts (assets, liabilities, equity) carry their balances forward.

wrongWhy.0: Asset and liability accounts are permanent — they are never closed and retain their balances.
wrongWhy.2: Revenue and dividend accounts are also closed, not just expense accounts.
wrongWhy.3: Cash is a permanent asset account that is never closed.

1-12.q: Which of the following is the correct sequence of closing entries?
o: Close expenses → Close revenues → Close Income Summary → Close dividends.
Close revenues → Close expenses → Close Income Summary → Close dividends.
Close dividends → Close revenues → Close expenses → Close Income Summary.
Close Income Summary → Close revenues → Close expenses → Close dividends.
1-12.e: The standard sequence is: (1) Close all revenue accounts to Income Summary, (2) Close all expense accounts to Income Summary, (3) Close Income Summary to Retained Earnings, (4) Close Dividends (or Drawings) to Retained Earnings.

wrongWhy.0: Expenses are closed before revenues in this option — the correct order is revenues first, then expenses.
wrongWhy.2: Dividends are closed last (directly to Retained Earnings), not first.
wrongWhy.3: Income Summary is closed third, after revenues and expenses have been transferred into it.

1-12.q: A company has a net loss of $12,000. What is the entry to close Income Summary to Retained Earnings?
o: Debit Income Summary $12,000; Credit Retained Earnings $12,000.
Debit Retained Earnings $12,000; Credit Income Summary $12,000.
Debit Income Summary $12,000; Credit Revenue $12,000.
No entry needed — a net loss does not affect Retained Earnings.
1-12.e: A net loss means expenses exceeded revenues, so Income Summary has a debit balance of $12,000. To close it, Retained Earnings is debited (reduced — a net loss decreases equity) and Income Summary is credited to zero it out.

wrongWhy.0: A net loss reduces Retained Earnings (debit RE), not increases it. Income Summary with a debit balance is closed by crediting it.
wrongWhy.2: Revenues were already closed in an earlier step. Income Summary is closed to Retained Earnings, not to Revenue.
wrongWhy.3: A net loss does reduce Retained Earnings — equity decreases by the amount of the loss.

1-12.q: Which statement is true about permanent accounts?
o: They are closed at year-end and restarted at zero.
They include revenues, expenses, and dividends.
Their balances carry forward from one period to the next.
They appear only on the income statement.
1-12.e: Permanent accounts (also called real accounts) are balance sheet accounts — assets, liabilities, and equity. Their balances are not reset to zero; they accumulate over the life of the business and carry forward into the next period.

wrongWhy.0: This describes temporary accounts, not permanent accounts. Permanent accounts are never closed.
wrongWhy.1: Revenues, expenses, and dividends are temporary accounts, not permanent accounts.
wrongWhy.3: Permanent accounts appear on the balance sheet, not the income statement.

1-12.q: What is the post-closing trial balance?
o: A trial balance prepared before adjusting entries.
A trial balance prepared after all closing entries have been posted.
A trial balance that lists only income statement accounts.
A trial balance used to prepare the statement of cash flows.
1-12.e: The post-closing trial balance is prepared after all closing entries have been posted to verify that temporary accounts have zero balances and that total debits still equal total credits. It contains only permanent account balances.

wrongWhy.0: A trial balance before adjusting entries is the unadjusted trial balance.
wrongWhy.2: The post-closing trial balance contains only permanent accounts (balance sheet accounts). All temporary (income statement) accounts should have zero balances.
wrongWhy.3: The post-closing trial balance verifies debit/credit equality after closing. It is not used to prepare the cash flow statement.

1-12.q: Dividends declared and paid during the year are closed by which entry?
o: Debit Retained Earnings; Credit Dividends.
Debit Dividends; Credit Income Summary.
Debit Income Summary; Credit Dividends.
Debit Dividends; Credit Retained Earnings.
1-12.e: The Dividends account (temporary) is closed by debiting Retained Earnings and crediting Dividends. This reduces Retained Earnings directly, reflecting that dividends are distributions of accumulated earnings to shareholders.

wrongWhy.1: Dividends are not closed through Income Summary — they are not part of net income calculation. They are closed directly to Retained Earnings.
wrongWhy.2: This would reduce Income Summary and increase Dividends — the opposite of closing the account.
wrongWhy.3: This is reversed. Retained Earnings is debited (decreased) and Dividends is credited (closed to zero).

1-12.q: If a company forgets to close its expense accounts at year-end, what is the effect on the next period's financial statements?
o: Next period's expenses will be understated.
Next period's net income will be overstated because expenses will not be re-accumulated.
Next period's expenses will be overstated because the prior period's balances remain in the accounts.
No effect — expense accounts reset automatically at period start.
1-12.e: If expenses are not closed, their balances carry forward into the next period. When new expenses are added to the existing balances, total expenses will be overstated, resulting in understated net income for the next period.

wrongWhy.0: Expenses would be overstated, not understated, because the prior period balances remain.
wrongWhy.1: Net income would be understated (because expenses are overstated), not overstated.
wrongWhy.3: Expense accounts do not reset automatically — closing entries must be made to zero them out.

1-12.q: After closing entries, Retained Earnings shows a balance of $95,000. During the period, revenues were $200,000, expenses were $155,000, and dividends were $10,000. What was the opening Retained Earnings balance?
1-12.e: Net income = $200,000 - $155,000 = $45,000. Ending RE = Beginning RE + Net income - Dividends. $95,000 = Beginning RE + $45,000 - $10,000. Beginning RE = $95,000 - $45,000 + $10,000 = $60,000.

wrongWhy.2: $50,000 does not satisfy the equation. Check: $50,000 + $45,000 - $10,000 = $85,000, not $95,000.
wrongWhy.3: $45,000 does not satisfy the equation. Check: $45,000 + $45,000 - $10,000 = $80,000, not $95,000.

1-13.q: After adjusting entries have been posted at the end of an accounting period, what is the next step in the accounting cycle?
o: Prepare an adjusted trial balance.
Prepare financial statements.
Prepare a post-closing trial balance.
Post the closing entries.
1-13.e: After adjusting entries have been posted to ledger accounts at the end of an accounting period, the next step in the 8-step accounting cycle is to prepare an adjusted trial balance that includes the adjusting entries. The eight steps in the accounting cycle are: (1) Identify financial events, gather details about them, and analyze them; (2) Record transactions in the general journal; (3) Post journalized transactions to ledger accounts; (4) Prepare an unadjusted trial balance; (5) Make adjusting entries; (6) Prepare an adjusted trial balance; (7) Prepare financial statements; (8) Prepare and post the closing entries and prepare a post-closing trial balance. Reversing entries for certain accruals are made at the beginning of the next accounting period.

wrongWhy.1: After adjusting entries have been posted to ledger accounts at the end of an accounting period, the next step in the 8-step accounting cycle is not to prepare financial statements. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.2: After adjusting entries have been posted to ledger accounts at the end of an accounting period, the next step in the 8-step accounting cycle is not to prepare a post-closing trial balance. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.3: After adjusting entries have been posted to ledger accounts at the end of an accounting period, the next step in the 8-step accounting cycle is not to post the closing entries. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-13.q: A company had the pre-closing trial balance at December 31 shown. Additional information: The balance of opening inventory was 140,000. The long-term debt pays interest at a rate of 10% per annum, payable every 12 months. The debt was issued on July 1 of the current year and originally had 5 years to maturity. The assets classified as property, plant, and equipment (exclusive of land) have a 10year estimated useful life and were 1 year old at the start of the current year. Straight-line depreciation is used by the company. Cash 80,000 Accounts receivable 100,000 Inventory 230,000 Gross property, plant, and equipment 600,000 Accumulated depreciation 60,000 Accounts payable 200,000 Long-term debt 1,000,000 Share capital 2,000,000 Retained earnings (Jan. 1) 500,000 Sales revenue 750,000 Purchases 530,000 Administrative expenses 200,000 Which is the correct order of the following four steps in the accounting cycle? Step 1 - make adjusting journal entries Step 2 - prepare a post-closing trial balance Step 3 - prepare an adjusted trial balance Step 4 - make reversing entries
1-13.e: The correct answer is: 1, 3, 2, 4. Review this topic in your study materials for a full explanation.

wrongWhy.0: The correct order of the accounting cycle is: (1) preparing adjusting journal entries, (2) preparing an adjusted trial balance, (3) preparing a post-closing trial balance, and (4) making reversing entries.
wrongWhy.2: The correct order of the accounting cycle is: (1) preparing adjusting journal entries, (2) preparing an adjusted trial balance, (3) preparing a post-closing trial balance, and (4) making reversing entries.
wrongWhy.3: The correct order of the accounting cycle is: (1) preparing adjusting journal entries, (2) preparing an adjusted trial balance, (3) preparng a post-closing trial balance, and (4) making reversing entries.

1-13.q: After journalized transactions have been posted to ledger accounts, what is the next step in the accounting cycle?
o: Prepare an adjusted trial balance
Prepare financial statements
Make adjusting entries
Prepare an unadjusted trial balance
1-13.e: After journalized transactions have been posted to ledger accounts, the next step in the 8-step accounting cycle is to prepare an unadjusted trial balance. The eight steps in the accounting cycle are: (1) Identify financial events, gather details about them, and analyze them; (2) Record transactions in the general journal; (3) Post journalized transactions to ledger accounts; (4) Prepare an unadjusted trial balance; (5) Make adjusting entries; (6) Prepare an adjusted trial balance; (7) Prepare financial statements; (8) Prepare and post the closing entries and prepare a post-closing trial balance. Reversing entries for certain accruals are made at the beginning of the next accounting period.

wrongWhy.0: After journalized transactions have been posted to ledger accounts, the next step in the 8-step accounting cycle is not to prepare an adjusted trial balance. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.1: After journalized transactions have been posted to ledger accounts, the next step in the 8-step accounting cycle is not to prepare financial statements. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.2: After journalized transactions have been posted to ledger accounts, the next step in the 8-step accounting cycle is not to make adjusting entries. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-13.q: Which of the following sets of accounting cycle steps is in the correct order?
o: Post journalized transactions to ledger accounts, prepare financial statements, prepare adjusted trial balance
Identify and analyze financial events, post journalized transactions to ledger accounts, record transactions in the general journal
Record transactions in the general journal, prepare unadjusted trial balance, prepare financial statements
Identify and analyze financial events, make adjusting entries, prepare unadjusted trial balance
1-13.e: These three steps are in the correct order. They are steps numbered 2, 4, and 7, in that order. The eight steps in the accounting cycle are: (1) Identify financial events, gather details about them, and analyze them; (2) Record transactions in the general journal; (3) Post journalized transactions to ledger accounts; (4) Prepare unadjusted trial balance; (5) Make adjusting entries; (6) Prepare adjusted trial balance; (7) Prepare financial statements; (8) Prepare and post the closing entries and prepare a post-closing trial balance. Reversing entries for certain accruals are made at the beginning of the next accounting period.

wrongWhy.0: These three steps are not in the correct order. They are steps numbered 3, 7, and 6, in that order. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.1: These three steps are not in the correct order. They are steps numbered 1, 3, and 2, in that order. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.3: These three steps are not in the correct order. They are steps numbered 1, 5, and 4, in that order. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-13.q: In addition to the 8-step accounting cycle, an additional activity that needs to be performed at the beginning of the next year’s accounting period is
o: Post the ending balances of balance sheet accounts to the accounts as their beginning balances for the next year.
Make any necessary corrections to the previous period’s financial statements.
Reset all income statement accounts to zero.
Post reversing entries to reverse any accruals for which a current transaction in the next period will duplicate the amount accrued during the previous year.
1-13.e: Reversing entries are generally posted as of the first day of the following accounting period for accruals that require them. Accruals that require them are those for which a current transaction in the next accounting period will duplicate the amount accrued during the previous year. That generally includes a sales invoice issued or a payables invoice received after some or all of the invoiced amount has already been accrued during the previous accounting period in order to recognize it in the correct accounting period according to accrual accounting.

wrongWhy.0: The balances of balance sheet accounts at the beginning of an accounting period are the same as the previous year’s ending balances, but there is no need to post them in the accounting system. Since balance sheet accounts (other than the Dividends contraequity account) are not closed out at the end of an accounting period, their balances carry over automatically to the next accounting period.
wrongWhy.1: This is not an additional activity that needs to be performed at the beginning of the next year’s accounting period.
wrongWhy.2: All income statement account balances are reset to zero in the year-end close, so there is no need to reset them to zero again at the beginning of the next accounting period.

1-13.q: Which of the following correctly lists the steps of the accounting cycle in order?
o: Post to ledger → Analyze transactions → Journalize → Prepare trial balance → Prepare financial statements → Adjust → Close.
Analyze transactions → Journalize → Post to ledger → Prepare unadjusted trial balance → Adjust → Prepare adjusted trial balance → Prepare financial statements → Close.
Journalize → Post to ledger → Adjust → Prepare trial balance → Prepare financial statements → Analyze → Close.
Prepare financial statements → Journalize → Post to ledger → Adjust → Close → Analyze transactions.
1-13.e: The accounting cycle in correct order: (1) Analyze transactions, (2) Journalize, (3) Post to ledger, (4) Unadjusted trial balance, (5) Adjusting entries, (6) Adjusted trial balance, (7) Financial statements, (8) Closing entries, (9) Post-closing trial balance.

wrongWhy.0: Posting to the ledger comes after journalizing, not before. Also, adjusting entries come before the final trial balance.
wrongWhy.2: Transactions must be analyzed before they can be journalized. Analysis is always the first step.
wrongWhy.3: Financial statements are prepared near the end of the cycle, after transactions have been journalized, posted, and adjusted.

1-13.q: What is the purpose of reversing entries?
o: To correct errors in adjusting entries.
To simplify the recording of certain cash transactions in the next period that relate to prior-period accruals.
To close temporary accounts at year-end.
To record transactions that were accidentally omitted during the period.
1-13.e: Reversing entries are optional entries made at the start of a new period that reverse certain adjusting entries from the prior period. Their purpose is to simplify bookkeeping — when the actual cash transaction is recorded, no allocation is needed because the reversing entry has already handled the prior-period portion.

wrongWhy.0: Correcting entries fix errors. Reversing entries are a planned, optional bookkeeping simplification tool.
wrongWhy.2: Closing entries zero out temporary accounts. Reversing entries are made at the start of the next period.
wrongWhy.3: Omitted transactions require correcting entries. Reversing entries are not used to record omissions.

1-13.q: Which of the following adjusting entries is most commonly reversed at the start of the next period?
o: Depreciation of long-term assets.
Expensing of prepaid insurance.
Accrued salaries payable at period-end.
Recognition of unearned revenue earned during the period.
1-13.e: Accrued expense adjusting entries (like salaries payable) are the most common candidates for reversal because they involve future cash payments. Reversing the entry at period start means when the full payroll is paid, the bookkeeper can simply record the full cash payment without needing to allocate between periods.

wrongWhy.0: Depreciation entries are not reversed because they do not involve future cash transactions that straddle periods.
wrongWhy.1: Prepaid expense adjustments are not typically reversed because the original payment has already been allocated.
wrongWhy.3: Deferred revenue adjustments are not typically reversed since the original cash receipt is already recorded correctly.

1-13.q: On December 31, a company recorded accrued wages of $3,000 (Debit Wages Expense; Credit Wages Payable). On January 1, a reversing entry is made. On January 10, the company pays $8,000 in wages for the full two-week period. What is the January 10 entry?
o: Debit Wages Expense $5,000; Debit Wages Payable $3,000; Credit Cash $8,000.
Debit Wages Expense $8,000; Credit Cash $8,000.
Debit Wages Payable $3,000; Debit Wages Expense $5,000; Credit Cash $8,000.
Debit Wages Expense $8,000; Credit Wages Payable $3,000; Credit Cash $5,000.
1-13.e: With a reversing entry on January 1 (Debit Wages Payable $3,000; Credit Wages Expense $3,000), Wages Expense has a credit balance of $3,000. When $8,000 is paid on January 10, debiting Wages Expense $8,000 and crediting Cash $8,000 results in a net expense of $5,000 for January — exactly the current period's portion. The reversing entry handles the December portion automatically.

wrongWhy.0: This entry is only needed without a reversing entry. With a reversing entry, the simple $8,000 debit to Wages Expense and credit to Cash achieves the correct result automatically.
wrongWhy.2: Same as option A — this manual allocation is unnecessary when a reversing entry has been made.
wrongWhy.3: With a reversing entry, the full $8,000 cash payment is debited to Wages Expense. No Wages Payable entry is needed because the reversing entry already cleared that liability.

1-13.q: Which of the following best describes a temporary account?
o: An account whose balance is carried forward indefinitely.
An account that is only used for one specific transaction.
An account whose balance is reset to zero at the end of each accounting period.
An account that records only non-cash transactions.
1-13.e: Temporary accounts (revenue, expense, and dividend accounts) are closed at the end of each accounting period. Their balances are transferred to Retained Earnings and the accounts start the next period at zero.

wrongWhy.0: This describes permanent accounts (assets, liabilities, equity), not temporary accounts.
wrongWhy.1: An account used for one transaction is not the definition of a temporary account. Temporary accounts are used throughout the period but closed at year-end.
wrongWhy.3: Temporary accounts record both cash and non-cash transactions — there is no such restriction.

1-13.q: What document is used immediately after the post-closing trial balance to begin the next accounting period?
o: The adjusted trial balance from the prior period.
A new general journal for the upcoming period.
The income statement from the prior period.
The unadjusted trial balance from the prior period.
1-13.e: After the post-closing trial balance confirms the books are ready, the company begins a new accounting cycle by recording transactions in a new general journal. The cycle starts again with analyzing and journalizing new transactions.

wrongWhy.0: The adjusted trial balance is prepared mid-cycle. At the start of a new period, the company begins recording new transactions.
wrongWhy.2: The prior income statement is a historical document — it does not initiate the new cycle.
wrongWhy.3: The unadjusted trial balance is prepared during the current cycle after posting, not at the start of a new period.

1-13.q: In the complete accounting cycle, at which point are financial statements prepared?
o: After the unadjusted trial balance, before adjusting entries.
After adjusting entries and the adjusted trial balance.
After closing entries.
Before posting to the general ledger.
1-13.e: Financial statements are prepared from the adjusted trial balance — after adjusting entries have been posted and an adjusted trial balance confirms debit/credit equality. The sequence is: adjusted trial balance → financial statements → closing entries.

wrongWhy.0: Financial statements prepared from the unadjusted trial balance would not properly reflect accruals, deferrals, and depreciation.
wrongWhy.2: Closing entries are made after financial statements are prepared. The statements drive what needs to be closed.
wrongWhy.3: Posting to the general ledger is an early step — financial statements come much later in the cycle.

1-13.q: A reversing entry on January 1 for accrued interest revenue of $500 would be recorded as:
o: Debit Interest Receivable $500; Credit Interest Revenue $500.
Debit Interest Revenue $500; Credit Interest Receivable $500.
Debit Cash $500; Credit Interest Revenue $500.
Debit Interest Expense $500; Credit Cash $500.
1-13.e: A reversing entry is the exact opposite of the original adjusting entry. The original entry was: Debit Interest Receivable $500; Credit Interest Revenue $500. The reversal on January 1 is: Debit Interest Revenue $500; Credit Interest Receivable $500.

wrongWhy.0: This is the original adjusting entry, not the reversal. A reversing entry is the mirror image of the original.
wrongWhy.2: Cash is not part of the reversing entry — cash will be recorded when actually received.
wrongWhy.3: Interest Expense applies when a company owes interest, not when it has earned interest.

1-13.q: Which of the following statements about reversing entries is correct?
o: Reversing entries are required by GAAP for all accrual-based companies.
Reversing entries must be made for depreciation adjustments.
Reversing entries are optional and used to simplify the recording of cash transactions in the new period.
Reversing entries always increase net income in the new period.
1-13.e: Reversing entries are entirely optional — they are a bookkeeping convenience, not a GAAP requirement. They simplify routine transactions in the new period by reversing certain accrual adjustments. They do not affect the ultimate accuracy of the financial statements.

wrongWhy.0: Reversing entries are not required by GAAP. They are an optional bookkeeping tool.
wrongWhy.1: Depreciation adjustments are never reversed because they do not involve future cash transactions that straddle periods.
wrongWhy.3: Reversing entries do not change the income for the new period — they simply shift the timing of certain entries without changing total recognized amounts.

1-13.q: How many formal trial balances are typically prepared in a complete accounting cycle?
o: One.
Two.
Three.
Four.
1-13.e: Three trial balances are prepared in a complete accounting cycle: (1) Unadjusted trial balance — after posting but before adjustments; (2) Adjusted trial balance — after adjusting entries; (3) Post-closing trial balance — after closing entries.

wrongWhy.0: One trial balance is insufficient — at minimum the adjusted and post-closing trial balances are needed.
wrongWhy.1: Two trial balances miss the unadjusted trial balance, which is an important check before making adjustments.
wrongWhy.3: Four trial balances would be excessive. The standard accounting cycle includes three formal trial balances.

1-13.q: Which of the following accounts would appear on a post-closing trial balance?
o: Service Revenue.
Depreciation Expense.
Retained Earnings.
Dividends Declared.
1-13.e: Retained Earnings is a permanent equity account and appears on the post-closing trial balance. Service Revenue, Depreciation Expense, and Dividends Declared are all temporary accounts that have been closed to zero and would not appear on the post-closing trial balance.

wrongWhy.0: Service Revenue is a temporary account closed to zero during the closing process. It would not appear.
wrongWhy.1: Depreciation Expense is a temporary account closed to zero during closing. It would not appear.
wrongWhy.3: Dividends Declared is a temporary account closed directly to Retained Earnings. It would not appear.

1-13.q: A company does NOT use reversing entries. On December 31, it accrued wages of $2,500. On January 8, it pays $6,000 for the full two-week payroll period. What is the January 8 journal entry?
o: Debit Wages Expense $6,000; Credit Cash $6,000.
Debit Wages Payable $2,500; Debit Wages Expense $3,500; Credit Cash $6,000.
Debit Wages Expense $3,500; Credit Wages Payable $2,500; Credit Cash $1,000.
Debit Wages Payable $6,000; Credit Cash $6,000.
1-13.e: Without reversing entries, the accountant must manually split the $6,000 payment: $2,500 settles the liability from December (debit Wages Payable $2,500) and $3,500 is the current period expense for January (debit Wages Expense $3,500). Total cash paid = $6,000.

wrongWhy.0: Recording the full $6,000 as January expense would double-count the $2,500 already expensed in December.
wrongWhy.2: Cash should be fully credited ($6,000), not partially. The credit to Cash must equal the full payment.
wrongWhy.3: The liability created was $2,500, not $6,000. Wages Payable should not be debited for $6,000.

1-13.q: Which step immediately follows the preparation of financial statements in the accounting cycle?
o: Preparing the adjusted trial balance.
Recording adjusting entries.
Recording closing entries.
Preparing the unadjusted trial balance.
1-13.e: The accounting cycle sequence near the end is: adjusted trial balance → financial statements → closing entries → post-closing trial balance. Closing entries are made immediately after financial statements are prepared.

wrongWhy.0: The adjusted trial balance is prepared before financial statements, not after.
wrongWhy.1: Adjusting entries are recorded before financial statements. They precede, not follow, the statements.
wrongWhy.3: The unadjusted trial balance is prepared early in the cycle, before adjustments and financial statements.

1-13.q: Why is the accounting cycle considered a 'cycle'?
o: Because it involves the same amounts each period.
Because it repeats continuously — each period's closing balances become the next period's opening balances.
Because revenues always cycle back to equal expenses over time.
Because the same journal entries are reused each accounting period.
1-13.e: The accounting cycle is a continuous, repeating process. Permanent account balances from one period (after closing) become the opening balances for the next period. The cycle of analyze → journalize → post → adjust → report → close repeats every accounting period.

wrongWhy.0: Amounts change every period based on the company's transactions — the cycle is the process that repeats, not the amounts.
wrongWhy.2: Revenues and expenses do not always equal each other — the cycle does not imply that revenues equal expenses.
wrongWhy.3: Journal entries differ each period based on unique transactions. The process is cyclical, not the specific entries.

1-13.q: What is the effect on the financial statements if closing entries are not made at year-end?
o: The balance sheet will be correct, but the income statement will be wrong.
The statement of cash flows will be incorrect.
Revenue and expense account balances from prior periods will accumulate, causing overstated income statement figures.
No effect — closing entries are optional and do not impact financial statements.
1-13.e: If closing entries are not made, temporary accounts carry their balances forward. Revenue and expense balances from prior periods accumulate, making the next period's income statement reflect multiple periods' results instead of just the current period.

wrongWhy.0: The balance sheet would also be affected — Retained Earnings would not reflect the transfer of net income or dividends from the current period.
wrongWhy.1: The cash flow statement is based on cash transactions during the period, not on temporary account balances.
wrongWhy.3: Closing entries are essential — without them, financial statements for subsequent periods will be materially misstated.

1-14.q: Which one of the following correctly describes the statement of shareholders’ equity?
o: It reconciles the beginning and ending balances in the retained earnings account.
It computes the number of shares outstanding used for earnings per share calculations.
It lists all shareholders’ equity accounts and their corresponding dollar amounts.
It reconciles the beginning and ending balances in shareholders’ equity accounts.
1-14.e: Thsi statement describes the statement of shareholders’ equity.

wrongWhy.0: The statement of shareholders’ equity reconciles the beginning and ending balances in all shareholders’ equity accounts, not just retained earnings.
wrongWhy.1: The statement of shareholders’ equity does not provide the information for the EPS calculations.
wrongWhy.2: The describes the statement of shareholders’ equity also provides the beginning of the period balances of the shareholders' equity accounts.

1-14.q: A company borrowed money from the bank to fund necessary inventory purchases. As a result, the company must pay the bank monthly interest of $5,241. This month, the company showed a gross profit of $17,934 and SG&A expenses of $9,964. The income tax rate for the company is 33%. Based on this information, what should the company report as its net income before taxes?
1-14.e: Net income before taxes is as follows: Gross profit $17,934 SG&A expenses 9,964 Operating income $ 7,970 Interest expense 5,241 Net income before tax $ 2,729

wrongWhy.1: This is gross profit, not net income before taxes. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.2: This is net income after tax, not net income before tax.
wrongWhy.3: This is operating income, not net income before taxes. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-14.q: A statement of financial position provides a basis for all of the following except
o: assessing liquidity and financial flexibility.
evaluating capital structure.
determining profitability and assessing past performance for a specific period.
computing rates of return.
1-14.e: A statement of financial position (balance sheet) cannot provide a basis for determining profitability and assessing past performance for a specific period. An income statement is required for that.

wrongWhy.0: A statement of financial position provides a basis for assessing liquidity and financial flexibility.
wrongWhy.1: A statement of financial position provides a basis for evaluating capital structure.
wrongWhy.3: A statement of financial position provides a basis for computing rates of return.

1-14.q: All of the following are components of comprehensive income except
o: revenue.
gains and losses.
expenses.
shareholders' equity.
1-14.e: This question is asking for the answer choice that is not a component of comprehensive income.. Shareholders' equity is not a component of comprehensive income. Components of comprehensive income are revenues, expenses, gains, and losses.

wrongWhy.0: This question is asking for the answer choice that is not a component of comprehensive income. Revenue is a component of comprehensive income. Components of comprehensive income are revenues, expenses, gains, and losses.
wrongWhy.1: This question is asking for the answer choice that is not a component of comprehensive income. Gains and losses are components of comprehensive income. Components of comprehensive income are revenues, expenses, gains, and losses.
wrongWhy.2: This question is asking for the answer choice that is not a component of comprehensive income. Expenses are a component of comprehensive income. Components of comprehensive income are revenues, expenses, gains, and losses.

1-14.q: The historical cost convention
o: has been replaced in accounting records by a system of current cost accounting.
fails to take into account changing price levels over time.
values all assets at their cost to the business, without any adjustment for depreciation.
records only past transactions.
1-14.e: "Historical cost" is the amount of cash, or its equivalent, that is paid to acquire an asset. It is generally used for property, plant and equipment, as well as most inventories. Historical cost may be adjusted after the acquisition for amortization or other allocations without changing the fact that historical cost is being used, because depreciation and amortization are methods of allocating the initial cost to the periods that will benefit from it. However, changes in fair value such as in market prices over time are not reflected in the carrying amount of an asset accounted for using historical cost.

wrongWhy.0: US GAAP is fundamentally a historical cost system of accounting, meaning that items are recorded in the books initially at their cost and then written down only over time. Items reported in financial statements are based on historical cost, fair value, current (replacement) cost, net realizable value, and present value of expected cash flows.
wrongWhy.2: "Historical cost" is the amount of cash, or its equivalent, that is paid to acquire an asset. It is generally used for property, plant and equipment, as well as most inventories. Historical cost may be adjusted after the acquisition for amortization or other allocations without changing the fact that historical cost is being used, because depreciation and amortization are methods of allocating the initial cost to the periods that will benefit from it.
wrongWhy.3: "Historical cost" is the amount of cash, or its equivalent, that is paid to acquire an asset. It is generally used for property, plant and equipment, as well as most inventories. Historical cost may be adjusted after the acquisition for amortization or other allocations without changing the fact that historical cost is being used, because depreciation and amortization are methods of allocating the initial cost to the periods that will benefit from it. Therefore, historical cost can involve current transactions as well as past transactions.

1-14.q: A company owns a building that still has a mortgage on it. What is the proper accounting classification for the building?
o: Current liability.
Long-term liability.
Long-term asset.
Current asset.
1-14.e: Assets are present rights of an entity to an economic benefit, or “what is owned,” so the building is an asset. The fact that it has a mortgage on it does not change its classification as an asset. Non-current assets, or long-term assets, are assets or resources other than those that are reasonably expected to be realized in cash or sold or consumed during the normal operating cycle of the business. A building is not expected to be liquidated for cash or consumed during the normal operating cycle of the business, so a building is a non-current, long-term asset.

wrongWhy.0: The building is not a current liability. Liabilities are present obligations of an entity to transfer or otherwise provide an economic benefit or benefits to others, or “what is owed,” so the building is not a liability at all, even though it has a mortgage on it. The mortgage is a liability, but the building is not a liability. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.1: The building is not a long-term liability. Liabilities are present obligations of an entity to transfer or otherwise provide an economic benefit or benefits to others, or “what is owed,” so the building is not a liability at all, even though it has a mortgage on it. The mortgage is a liability, but the building is not a liability. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.3: Assets are present rights of an entity to an economic benefit, or “what is owned,” so the building is an asset. The fact that it has a mortgage on it does not change its classification as an asset. However, current assets are cash and other assets or resources that are reasonably expected to be realized in cash or sold or consumed during the normal operating cycle of the business. A building is not expected to be liquidated for cash or consumed during the normal operating cycle of the business, so a building is not a current asset. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-14.q: The primary objective of financial statement reporting is to meet the needs of
o: officers and auditors.
staff and customers.
supervisors and managers.
investors and creditors.
1-14.e: The objective of financial reporting is to provide useful financial information about the entity for decision-making. Those using the financial information to make decisions include present and potential equity investors, lenders, and other creditors who need to make decisions about providing resources to the entity. The decisions relate to buying, selling, or holding debt or equity instruments and providing credit. The investors, lenders, and other creditors need information that will help them assess the amount of, timing of, and prospects for future net cash inflows to the entity to make their decisions. Other users who may or may not be providing capital to the firm—such as management, employees, financial analysts, and regulators—may find financial statements useful as well.

wrongWhy.0: The objective of financial reporting is to provide useful financial information about the entity for decision-making. Those using the financial information to make decisions include present and potential equity investors, lenders, and other creditors who need to make decisions about providing resources to the entity. Officers may find the financial statements useful as well, but they are not the primary users for whom financial statements are prepared. Auditors audit the financial statements prepared by manaagement, but the financial statements are not prepared to meet the needs of auditors.
wrongWhy.1: The objective of financial reporting is to provide useful financial information about the entity for decision-making. Those using the financial information to make decisions include present and potential equity investors, lenders, and other creditors who need to make decisions about providing resources to the entity. Staff and customers may find the financial statements useful as well, but they are not the primary users for whom financial statements are prepared.
wrongWhy.2: The objective of financial reporting is to provide useful financial information about the entity for decision-making by present and potential equity investors, lenders, and other creditors who need to make decisions about providing resources to the entity. Supervisors and manageers may find the financial statements useful as well, but they are not the primary users for whom financial statements are prepared.

1-14.q: Which one of the following financial statements best reflects the accounting equation?
o: Statement of cash flows.
Income statement.
Audit report.
Balance sheet.
1-14.e: The balance sheet best reflects the accounting equation. The accounting equation is Assets = Liabilities + Equity, and the balance sheet provides information about an entity’s assets, liabilities, and owners’ equity as of a specific date (usually the end of a reporting period).

wrongWhy.0: The statement of cash flows does not reflect the accounting equation. The accounting equation is Assets = Liabilities + Equity, and those items are not presented on the statement of cash flows. A statement of cash flows provides information regarding cash receipts and cash payments made by an entity during a period. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.1: The income statement does not reflect the accounting equation. The accounting equation is Assets = Liabilities + Equity, and those items are not presented on the income statement. The income statement reports the results of a company’s operations during a given period of time. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.2: An audit report does not reflect the accounting equation. The accounting equation is Assets = Liabilities + Equity, and those items are not presented in an audit report. An audit report is usually a report prepared by an independent auditor after completing an audit, and, among other things, it contains the auditor’s opinion on whether the audited company’s financial statements present fairly, in all material respects, the financial position of the company, the results of its operations, and its cash flows for the years audited. An audit report does not contain financial statements, but rather an audit report is contained in an annual report published by a company. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-14.q: A company has decided to include certain financial ratios in its year-end annual report to shareholders. Selected information relating to its most recent fiscal year is provided below. Cash $ 10,000 Accounts receivable 20,000 Prepaid expenses 8,000 Inventory 42,000 Accounts payable 15,000 Notes payable (due in 90 days) 25,000 Bonds payable (due in 10 years) 35,000 Net credit sales for year 220,000 Cost of goods sold 140,000 The company’s net working capital at year end is
1-14.e: The following items are included in the claculation of working capital: Cash Accounts receivable 20,000 Prepaid expenses 8,000 Inventory 42,000 Accounts payable (15,000) Notes payable (due in 90 days) (25,000) $ 10,000 This totals working capital of $40,000.

wrongWhy.0: This answer is incorrect. See the correct answer for a complete explanation.
wrongWhy.1: This answer is incorrect. See the correct answer for a complete explanation.
wrongWhy.2: This answer is incorrect. See the correct answer for a complete explanation.

1-14.q: The financial statement that provides a summary of the firm’s operations for a period of time is the
o: statement of financial position.
statement of shareholders’ equity.
statement of retained earnings.
income statement.
1-14.e: The income statement reports the results of operations for a period of time.

wrongWhy.0: The statement of financial position does not report the results of operations for a period of time.
wrongWhy.1: The statement of shareholders’ equity does not report the results of operations for a period of time.
